Back to Table of Contents
02
Understanding Cooperative Types and Choosing the Right One

Chapter 2 — Understanding Cooperative Types and Choosing the Right One

The first decision you make about your cooperative is the most consequential. Choose the wrong type and you will spend years fighting a structure that does not match your community’s needs. You will overpay for compliance. You will confuse your members. You will build governance for activities you never pursue while neglecting the ones that matter.

A women’s savings group in Lanao del Sur registered as a multi-purpose cooperative because the organizer assumed bigger was better. The group wanted one thing: a credit facility for small loans among its thirty members. Instead, they inherited the governance burden of a multi-purpose structure — mandatory committees for lending, marketing, and production they never intended to operate, capital requirements nearly seven times higher than necessary, and reporting obligations that overwhelmed their part-time treasurer. Within two years, they lost their certificate of compliance. They joined the 88.34 percent of BARMM cooperatives that cannot prove they meet minimum legal requirements.1

That outcome was preventable. This chapter gives you the tools to prevent it.

2.1 Types of Cooperatives Under Philippine and Bangsamoro Law

Two legal frameworks define cooperative types in BARMM. RA 9520 (the Philippine Cooperative Code of 2008) applies today and governs all cooperatives registered with CSEA.2 BTA Bill No. 210 (the proposed Bangsamoro Cooperative and Social Enterprise Code) will replace many of these provisions once enacted.3 The good news: both frameworks recognize substantially the same cooperative types. The differences lie in the details — and in the addition of social enterprises under BTA Bill No. 210.

Know both frameworks. Build your cooperative under RA 9520 today. Prepare for BTA Bill No. 210 tomorrow.

2.1.1 The Twenty-One Types Recognized by Law

RA 9520 identifies the primary cooperative types under Article 23. BTA Bill No. 210, Section 20 expands the list to twenty-one explicit types — including several that RA 9520 recognized implicitly but did not name individually.4 Here is the complete list, drawn from both frameworks.

1. Credit Cooperative

Promotes and undertakes savings and lending services among members. Generates a common pool of funds to provide financial assistance for productive and provident purposes.5 This is the backbone of cooperative finance in BARMM.

Example: A group of market vendors in Cotabato City pools weekly savings and extends small loans to members for inventory restocking.

2. Consumers Cooperative

Procures and distributes commodities to members and non-members at fair prices.6 Think of it as a community-owned store.

Example: A barangay cooperative in Basilan bulk-purchases rice, canned goods, and school supplies, selling to members at lower prices than retail.

3. Producers Cooperative

Undertakes joint production — agricultural or industrial. Members produce raw materials or goods; the cooperative processes and sells them.7 This is the most common type in BARMM, accounting for 37.58 percent of all registered cooperatives in the region.8

Example: Cassava farmers in Maguindanao del Norte collectively process their harvest into cassava chips and flour for sale to distributors. BARMM produces 41.70 percent of the country’s cassava supply.9

4. Marketing Cooperative

Supplies production inputs to members and markets their products.10 The critical distinction from a producers cooperative: a marketing cooperative does not process goods. It connects producers to buyers.

Example: A seaweed marketing cooperative in Tawi-Tawi aggregates dried seaweed from individual farmers and negotiates bulk contracts with processors in Zamboanga. BARMM produces 48.42 percent of the Philippines’ seaweed.11 Marketing cooperatives account for 27.54 percent of all cooperatives in the region.12

5. Service Cooperative

Engages in medical and dental care, hospitalization, transportation, insurance, housing, labor, power and electricity, communication, professional services, and other services.13 This is the broadest single-purpose type.

Example: A transport cooperative in Lanao del Sur operates multicabs along routes where no public utility vehicles run.

6. Multi-Purpose Cooperative

Combines two or more of the business activities of the other types.14 This is the most recognized type nationwide — and the most frequently misused. See Section 2.2.2 for why multi-purpose is not always the answer.

Example: A cooperative in Maguindanao del Sur that operates a lending window, a consumer store, and a rice trading business under one registration.

7. Advocacy Cooperative

Promotes and advocates cooperativism among members and the public through socially-oriented projects, education, training, research, and communication.15 This type does not engage in commercial business. Its mission is to strengthen the cooperative movement itself.

Example: A cooperative development advocacy group that conducts Pre-Registration Seminars and cooperative governance training in island municipalities of Tawi-Tawi.

8. Agrarian Reform Cooperative

Organized by marginal farmers, the majority of whom are agrarian reform beneficiaries, for developing appropriate systems of land tenure, land development, land consolidation, or land management in areas covered by agrarian reform.16 BARMM has 240 Agrarian Reform Beneficiary Organizations (ARBOs) with 34,743 members across the region.17

Example: Rice farmers in Lanao del Sur who received Certificates of Land Ownership Award (CLOAs) form a cooperative to consolidate their small plots for mechanized farming.

9. Cooperative Bank

Organized primarily to provide a wide range of financial services to cooperatives and their members.18 This is a secondary or tertiary cooperative — its members are other cooperatives, not individuals. Cooperative banks are regulated by the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP) in addition to CSEA.

Example: Five credit cooperatives in Maguindanao pool resources to form a cooperative bank that can offer checking accounts, remittance services, and larger loan facilities.

10. Dairy Cooperative

Members are engaged in the production of fresh milk, which may be processed and marketed as dairy products.19

Example: Carabao dairy farmers in a cluster of barangays organize to produce pasteurized milk and yogurt for sale in local markets.

11. Education Cooperative

Organized for the primary purpose of owning and operating licensed educational institutions.20 BTA Bill No. 210 explicitly references the Bangsamoro Education Code alongside RA 9155 (Governance of Basic Education Act).

Example: A community in Basilan establishes a cooperative that owns and operates a private elementary and high school, with tuition revenues reinvested into school facilities.

12. Electric Cooperative

Undertakes power generation (using renewable energy sources, including hybrid systems), acquisition, and operation of sub-transmission or distribution to household members.21

Example: An off-grid island community in Tawi-Tawi forms an electric cooperative to install and manage solar panel systems for member households.

13. Financial Service Cooperative

Organized for savings and credit services and other financial services — broader than a credit cooperative but without the full banking license of a cooperative bank.22 Requires a minimum paid-up capital of PHP 10,000,000.23

Example: A large cooperative in Cotabato City that accepts deposits, issues loans, and offers remittance services but does not operate as a bank.

14. Fishermen Cooperative

Organized by marginalized fishermen whose products are marketed as fresh or processed products.24 This type exists alongside producers cooperatives but targets the specific needs of fishing communities.

Example: Small-scale fishermen in island barangays of Basilan collectively own motorized bancas, share fuel costs, and market their daily catch through the cooperative.

15. Health Services Cooperative

Organized for medical, dental, and other health services.25

Example: Health professionals and community members in Lanao del Sur form a cooperative to operate a community health clinic offering consultations, laboratory services, and a pharmacy.

16. Housing Cooperative

Organized to assist or provide access to housing for regular members who actively participate in the savings program for housing.26 Co-owned and controlled by members.

Example: A group of government employees in Cotabato City forms a housing cooperative to develop an affordable residential subdivision for its members.

17. Insurance Cooperative

Engaged in the business of insuring life and property of cooperatives and their members.27

Example: A federation of cooperatives in BARMM establishes an insurance cooperative to offer affordable life and calamity insurance products to member-cooperatives.

18. Transport Cooperative

Includes land and sea transportation, limited to small vessels as defined under Philippine maritime laws.28

Example: Motorela operators in a municipality of Lanao del Sur organize as a transport cooperative, pooling resources for vehicle maintenance and negotiating franchise terms collectively.

19. Water Service Cooperative

Organized to own, operate, and manage water systems for the provision and distribution of potable water to members and their households.29 Requires a minimum paid-up capital of PHP 1,000,000.30

Example: Households in a rural barangay of Maguindanao del Sur form a water service cooperative to develop and maintain a spring-fed water distribution system.

20. Workers Cooperative

Organized by workers, including the self-employed, who are simultaneously the members and owners of the enterprise. The principal purpose is to provide employment and business opportunities to members.31

Example: Weavers producing traditional Maranao malong organize a workers cooperative where each member is both an artisan and a co-owner of the weaving enterprise.

21. Other Types

Both RA 9520 and BTA Bill No. 210 include a catch-all provision: other types of cooperatives as may be determined by the Authority.32 This gives CSEA the flexibility to recognize new cooperative forms as the economy evolves.

2.1.2 Categories by Membership Size and Asset Level

Beyond type, every cooperative falls into a category that determines its regulatory obligations. Two classification systems apply: one based on organizational level and one based on asset size.

Organizational Level

Category Who Are the Members? Minimum to Organize
Primary Natural persons (individuals) 15 persons
Secondary (Federation) Primary cooperatives 3 cooperatives (BTA Bill 210) / 2 cooperatives (RA 9520)
Tertiary (Union) Secondary cooperatives 2 secondaries

Most cooperatives you will encounter in BARMM are primary cooperatives — their members are individual people. When primary cooperatives band together, they form secondaries (federations). When federations organize at a higher level, they form tertiaries (unions). BTA Bill No. 210 requires a minimum of three registered cooperatives to form a federation.33 RA 9520 requires two.34

Asset Size Categories

CDA classifies cooperatives by total asset size. This classification determines audit requirements, reporting standards, and eligibility for certain programs.35

Category Total Assets What This Means
Micro Up to ₱3,000,000 Internal audit by Audit Committee is sufficient
Small ₱3,000,001 to ₱15,000,000 CPA audit required (may be CDA-accredited)
Medium ₱15,000,001 to ₱100,000,000 CPA audit required; enhanced reporting
Large Above ₱100,000,000 CPA audit by CDA-accredited auditor; full compliance regime

Why does category matter? Three reasons.

First, audit requirements scale with category. A micro cooperative with assets under ₱3 million can rely on its own Audit Committee for the annual audit.36 A large cooperative with assets exceeding ₱100 million must engage a CDA-accredited external CPA. The cost difference is significant for small communities.

Second, reporting obligations increase with size. Larger cooperatives face more granular reporting requirements in their Cooperative Annual Progress Report (CAPR) and Cooperative Audited Financial Statements (CAFS). If you register as a type that forces you into a higher asset category before you are ready, you inherit compliance burdens that can overwhelm your officers.

Third, training mandates vary. Officers of larger cooperatives face stricter training requirements. The CDA and CSEA mandate specific governance and management courses for officers based on cooperative category.

Territorial Categories

BTA Bill No. 210 also categorizes cooperatives by territory — their area of operations, which may or may not coincide with political subdivisions.37 A cooperative’s area of operation is defined in its Articles of Cooperation. Start local. Expand only when your membership and capacity justify it.

2.2 Matching Your Community’s Needs to a Cooperative Type

You now know the twenty-one types and the categories. The harder question is: which type fits your community?

Do not start with the type. Start with the need.

2.2.1 The Five Questions to Ask Before Choosing a Type

Before you select a cooperative type, gather your prospective members and answer five questions honestly. Write the answers down. They will shape every decision that follows.

Question 1: What is the primary economic activity of your members?

Identify the single activity that most members share. Are they farmers? Fishermen? Market vendors? Weavers? Transport operators? The answer usually points directly to a cooperative type.

If most members are seaweed farmers, you are looking at a producers cooperative or a marketing cooperative — not a multi-purpose cooperative. If most members are salaried workers who need savings and loan facilities, you are looking at a credit cooperative.

Question 2: What do your members need most right now?

Separate wants from needs. A community may want a cooperative store, a lending window, and a rice trading business. But what do they need first? If the answer is access to credit, start with credit. If the answer is fair prices for their produce, start with marketing.

The temptation is to say “we need everything.” Resist that temptation. A cooperative that tries to do everything from day one does nothing well.

Question 3: How much capital can your members realistically raise?

Be honest about this. The minimum paid-up capital for a primary single-purpose cooperative is ₱15,000.38 For a multi-purpose cooperative, it is ₱100,000.39 For a financial service cooperative, it is ₱10,000,000.40 For a cooperative bank, the BSP sets additional capital requirements.

If your fifteen members can each contribute ₱1,000, you have ₱15,000 — enough for a primary single-purpose cooperative but far short of a multi-purpose structure. Do not overcommit your members. Start with a type your community can afford.

Cooperative Type Minimum Paid-Up Capital If 15 Members Share Equally
Primary (general single-purpose) ₱15,000 ₱1,000 per member
Multi-purpose ₱100,000 ₱6,667 per member
Credit ₱100,000 ₱6,667 per member
Water Service ₱1,000,000 ₱66,667 per member
Financial Service ₱10,000,000 ₱666,667 per member
Electric ₱5,000,000 ₱333,333 per member

Look at the last column. If your members are marginalized fisherfolk in Tawi-Tawi earning subsistence-level incomes, a financial service cooperative is not realistic. A fishermen cooperative at ₱15,000 minimum paid-up is.

Question 4: What regulatory burden can your officers manage?

Every cooperative type carries compliance obligations: annual general assemblies, CAPR and CAFS submissions, audit requirements, committee formations, training mandates. Multi-purpose cooperatives carry the heaviest burden because they must comply with the rules for every business activity they operate.

Ask yourself: Do your prospective officers have the time, literacy, and bookkeeping skills to handle complex compliance? If your officers are volunteer farmers in a remote barangay, a simpler cooperative type with fewer reporting requirements may serve you better.

Question 5: What infrastructure, organizations, and markets already exist in your community?

Do not duplicate what already exists. If a strong credit cooperative already operates in your area, your community may benefit more from a marketing cooperative that helps members sell their products — and encourages them to join the existing credit cooperative for their savings and lending needs.

Map what exists. Find the gap. Fill that gap with the right cooperative type.

2.2.2 Common Mistakes: Why Multi-Purpose Is Not Always the Answer

Walk into any CSEA field office and ask which cooperative type people choose most often. The answer is multi-purpose. Ask which type has the highest failure rate. The answer is often the same.

Multi-purpose is the default because it sounds safe. If you register as multi-purpose, you can do credit, production, marketing, and consumer services all under one roof. Why limit yourself?

Here is why.

Higher capital requirements. The minimum paid-up capital for a multi-purpose cooperative is ₱100,000 — nearly seven times higher than the ₱15,000 required for a primary single-purpose cooperative.41 For a community of fifteen members with limited savings, that difference can mean the cooperative never forms at all, or forms with members overextending themselves financially.

More complex governance. A multi-purpose cooperative must form committees for each business activity. If you operate a lending window and a consumer store, you need a credit committee, an inventory committee, and the management capacity to oversee both. Each committee needs trained officers. Each business line needs its own bookkeeping. The governance overhead multiplies with every additional business activity.

Heavier compliance burden. Multi-purpose cooperatives must report on all their business activities in their CAPR and CAFS. Their audited financial statements must cover multiple revenue streams, cost centers, and fund allocations. For a micro cooperative with a part-time treasurer, this level of reporting is often the reason compliance lapses — and compliance lapses are the reason cooperatives lose their certificates.

Diluted focus. The most successful cooperatives in BARMM do one thing well before they expand. A seaweed marketing cooperative in Tawi-Tawi that builds a reliable supply chain for its one product creates more value than a multi-purpose cooperative in the same community that spreads its limited capital across lending, trading, and retail — doing all three poorly.

When is multi-purpose the right choice? When your cooperative has:

  • Enough members (well beyond the minimum 15) to staff multiple committees
  • Sufficient capital to fund multiple business activities without straining any one
  • Officers with proven governance and bookkeeping experience
  • Existing operations in two or more areas that genuinely complement each other
  • The explicit demand from members for multiple services

If you cannot check all five, start single-purpose. You can always amend your Articles of Cooperation later to add business activities once your cooperative is stable and compliant.42

2.3 Single-Purpose vs. Multi-Purpose: Trade-offs

The choice between single-purpose and multi-purpose is the most consequential structural decision you will make. Here are the trade-offs laid out clearly.

2.3.1 Regulatory Requirements by Type

Requirement Single-Purpose (e.g., Producers) Multi-Purpose
Regulatory authority CSEA (BARMM) / CDA (national) CSEA / CDA
Minimum membership 15 natural persons 15 natural persons
Minimum paid-up capital ₱15,000 ₱100,000
Mandatory committees Board of Directors, Audit Committee, Election Committee, Mediation/Conciliation Committee All of the above PLUS committees for each business line (e.g., Credit Committee, Education and Training Committee)
Annual reporting CAPR + CAFS for one business activity CAPR + CAFS covering ALL business activities
Audit requirement (Micro) Internal by Audit Committee Internal by Audit Committee
Audit requirement (Small+) External CPA External CPA
General Assembly Annual, within 90 days after fiscal year end Same
Training mandate Officers must complete mandatory training per CDA/CSEA rules Same, plus training for each business-line committee

2.3.2 Capital Requirements by Type

Use this table to determine how much capital your cooperative needs based on type and the capital structure rules under RA 9520 and CDA Memorandum Circular 2015-01 (as amended by MC 2016-02).43

Capital Structure Formula: - Authorized Capital = Total shares the cooperative can issue (set by organizers) - Subscribed Capital = At least 25% of Authorized Capital - Paid-Up Capital = At least 25% of Subscribed Capital (subject to minimum floor)

Cooperative Type Minimum Paid-Up Capital Capital to Authorize (Working Backward)
Primary (single-purpose) ₱15,000 ₱240,000
Multi-Purpose ₱100,000 ₱1,600,000
Credit ₱100,000 ₱1,600,000
Water Service ₱1,000,000 ₱16,000,000
Electric ₱5,000,000 ₱80,000,000
Financial Service ₱10,000,000 ₱160,000,000
Cooperative Bank Per BSP regulations Per BSP regulations
Secondary (Federation) ₱100,000 ₱1,600,000
Tertiary (Union) ₱100,000 ₱1,600,000

How to read the “Working Backward” column: If the minimum paid-up is ₱15,000, and paid-up must be at least 25% of subscribed, then subscribed must be at least ₱60,000. And if subscribed must be at least 25% of authorized, then authorized must be at least ₱240,000. Organizers set the authorized capital in the Articles of Cooperation — so plan accordingly.

Example for a Producers Cooperative:

A cassava producers cooperative in Maguindanao del Norte has 20 members. They set the authorized capital at ₱300,000 (with a par value of ₱1,000 per share, 300 shares). Each member subscribes to 4 shares (₱4,000 each), for a total subscribed capital of ₱80,000 — this exceeds 25% of ₱300,000 (₱75,000). Each member pays 25% of their subscription upfront (₱1,000), for a total paid-up capital of ₱20,000 — this exceeds the ₱15,000 minimum floor.

The cooperative is now capitalized, compliant, and ready to register as a primary producers cooperative.

2.4 Federations, Unions, and Secondary Cooperatives

Individual cooperatives are powerful. Cooperatives working together are transformative. Philippine and Bangsamoro law recognizes higher-level cooperative structures designed for exactly this purpose.

2.4.1 When Your Cooperative Should Join a Federation

A federation is a secondary cooperative whose members are primary cooperatives of the same type or related business activities.44 Think of a federation as a cooperative of cooperatives.

Minimum requirement under BTA Bill No. 210: Three or more registered cooperatives in BARMM of the same type of services may form a federation.45 Under RA 9520, the minimum is two.46

What can a federation do?

Function How It Helps Your Cooperative
Carry on complementary business Provides services that augment your cooperative’s work without competing with it
Education and advisory work Conducts trainings, seminars, and capability-building for member cooperatives
Simplify operations Offers shared bookkeeping, accounting systems, and operational procedures
Publish and communicate Produces newsletters, market information, and educational materials
Coordinate activities Harmonizes schedules, standards, and market access across members
Enter joint ventures Negotiates with national and international partners on behalf of all members

When should you consider forming or joining a federation?

  • When your cooperative cannot negotiate good prices alone because your volume is too small. Five seaweed marketing cooperatives in Tawi-Tawi negotiating as a federation have far more leverage with processors than any single cooperative.
  • When your officers need training but cannot afford to bring in trainers individually. A federation can organize and subsidize trainings for all member cooperatives.
  • When shared services (bookkeeping, audit preparation, compliance filing) would save money for every member cooperative.
  • When you need to advocate collectively with CSEA, LGUs, or the BTA Parliament for policies that affect your sector.

Capital requirement for federations: Minimum paid-up capital of ₱100,000.47

2.4.2 Cooperative Unions and Their Functions

A cooperative union is different from a federation. Where federations are organized by cooperatives of the same type and engage in business activities, unions represent the interests and welfare of all types of cooperatives at the provincial, city, regional, or national level.48

Unions are primarily advocacy and representation bodies, not business enterprises. Under BTA Bill No. 210, cooperative unions may:49

  • Represent member organizations before government and other bodies
  • Acquire, analyze, and disseminate economic and statistical information
  • Sponsor studies in the economic, legal, financial, and social aspects of cooperation
  • Promote cooperative principles and practices
  • Develop the cooperative movement in their jurisdictions
  • Advise authorities on all questions relating to cooperatives
  • Raise funds through membership fees, dues, donations, and subsidies

Key distinction: A federation does business. A union does advocacy. A federation of fishermen cooperatives might market fish collectively. A cooperative union might lobby the BTA Parliament for tax incentives for all cooperatives.

When should cooperatives form or join a union?

  • When cooperatives of different types in a province or city need a unified voice
  • When the cooperative sector needs representation before CSEA, the BTA Parliament, or national agencies
  • When policy advocacy — not shared business activity — is the primary goal

Cooperative unions may assist national, regional, and local governments in development activities within their jurisdictions.50 In a region where most LGUs have no appointed Municipal Cooperative Officers, a strong cooperative union can fill the gap between CSEA’s regional office and the cooperatives in remote municipalities.51

2.5 Social Enterprises: A Parallel Path

BTA Bill No. 210 does something that RA 9520 does not: it creates a legal framework for social enterprises alongside cooperatives.52 Book III of the proposed code introduces social enterprises as a distinct organizational form — and as a status that cooperatives themselves can adopt.

If you are organizing a community-based initiative in BARMM, you now have two paths, not just one. Understanding the difference — and the overlap — is critical.

2.5.1 What Makes a Social Enterprise Different from a Cooperative

A social enterprise (SE) under BTA Bill No. 210 is a business organization primarily dedicated to addressing social, cultural, or environmental challenges, with profit as a secondary consideration.53

A cooperative is an autonomous association of persons with a common bond who voluntarily join together to achieve their social, economic, and cultural needs by making equitable contributions to capital and sharing risks and benefits.54

The distinction is subtle but important:

Feature Cooperative Social Enterprise
Primary purpose Meet members’ shared needs Address social, cultural, or environmental challenges
Profit orientation Benefits distributed to members (patronage refunds, interest on capital) Profit is secondary; revenues reinvested in social mission
Governance Democratic member control (one member, one vote) Democratic governance with stakeholder inclusion (not limited to member-owners)
Who benefits Members primarily The marginalized sector served or society at large
Legal form Cooperative (registered with CSEA/CDA) May be a corporation, partnership, sole proprietorship, or cooperative
Regulatory body CSEA (BARMM) / CDA (national) CSEA (BARMM) for registration and accreditation

The most important line in that table: a social enterprise can take any legal form — including a cooperative.55 A cooperative can also be a social enterprise. The two are not mutually exclusive.

Social enterprises under BTA Bill No. 210 may operate in areas including:56

  • Social services
  • Health care
  • Education
  • Livelihood development
  • Environmental protection
  • Peace and reconciliation
  • Disaster risk reduction and management

2.5.2 When to Organize as a Social Enterprise Instead

Choose a social enterprise over a cooperative when:

Your primary mission is to serve people who are not your members. A cooperative exists to serve its members. A social enterprise exists to serve a target community or address a societal problem. If your initiative aims to provide free vocational training to out-of-school youth, or to rehabilitate mangrove ecosystems, or to offer subsidized health services to internally displaced persons — and the beneficiaries are not the same people who will own and govern the organization — a social enterprise structure may fit better.

Your funding model relies on reinvestment rather than member returns. Cooperatives distribute net surplus to members through patronage refunds and interest on capital. Social enterprises invest a substantive part of their net revenues back into their social mission.57 If your organizers want the profits to go back into the community rather than into member dividends, the SE model aligns better.

Your governance includes stakeholders beyond member-owners. Social enterprises must apply rules of democratic governance where decision-making includes relevant stakeholders in addition to share owners or members.58 If your organization wants beneficiaries, partner NGOs, or community leaders involved in governance alongside investor-members, the SE structure accommodates this.

Can you be both? Yes. BTA Bill No. 210 explicitly provides that cooperatives may be registered and accredited as social enterprises.59 A cooperative operating as a social enterprise enjoys the tax treatment provided under the Cooperative Code for its cooperative activities, plus additional benefits for its social enterprise status.60

Tax advantages for social enterprises under BTA Bill No. 210:

SE Category Tax Benefit
Micro SEs Exempt from tax on income from operations; LGUs may reduce or exempt from local taxes61
Other SEs Exempt from regional taxes, fees, and charges for the first three years of operation; LGUs may reduce or exempt from local taxes62
Cooperatives operating as SEs Covered by cooperative tax treatment under the Code63
All qualified SEs Preferential rights in government procurement processes64
SE reinvestments Reinvestments for SE initiatives within the area of operation are tax deductible from gross income65

The dual-registration advantage. A cooperative that also registers as a social enterprise under CSEA can access both cooperative support programs (PESO Project, STOCK Project) and social enterprise programs (RISE for Peace and Sustainable Development, Social Enterprise Capability Building and Sustainability Program).66 This is a strategic option for cooperatives whose work has a clear social mission beyond member benefit.

Financing for social enterprises. BTA Bill No. 210 mandates a Shari’ah-compliant financing program for startup and micro cooperatives and social enterprises, implemented by CSEA in collaboration with the Ministry of Finance and Budget and Management (MFBM), the Ministry of Agriculture, Fisheries, and Aquatic Resources (MAFAR), and the Ministry of Trade, Investments, and Tourism (MTIT).67 This financing must be paired with business-readiness and capacity development interventions.68

Local government units must allocate five percent of their funds toward financing social enterprise initiatives prioritized in local development plans.69 For communities in BARMM where access to conventional financing is limited, this provision opens a meaningful funding channel.

Choosing Your Type: A Decision Summary

Use this flowchart as a final check before you commit to a cooperative type.

Step 1: Identify the primary activity. Ask: What is the one thing your members do, need, or produce? Match it to one of the twenty-one types.

Step 2: Check the capital requirement. Can your members realistically raise the minimum paid-up capital for that type? If not, consider a type with a lower threshold — or recruit more members to spread the cost.

Step 3: Assess your governance capacity. Do you have enough capable, willing officers to staff the mandatory committees? If not, choose a simpler structure.

Step 4: Evaluate multi-purpose honestly. Only register as multi-purpose if you genuinely need two or more business activities from day one AND you have the capital, members, and officers to manage them all.

Step 5: Consider social enterprise status. If your mission extends beyond member benefit to community impact, explore dual registration as a cooperative and social enterprise under BTA Bill No. 210.

Step 6: Plan to grow. Start simple. Register as a single-purpose cooperative. Build compliance discipline. Train your officers. Grow your membership and capital. When you are ready — and only when you are ready — amend your Articles of Cooperation to add new business activities or convert to multi-purpose.

The best cooperative for your community is not the biggest or the most versatile. It is the one your community can sustain.

  1. BDP 2023-2028, Chapter 7. Of 12,214 cooperatives registered in the BARMM between 2010 and 2020, only 1,424 (11.66%) secured compliance certificates. ↩

Back to Table of Contents
03
From Idea to Organizing Group: Pre-Formation

Chapter 3 — From Idea to Organizing Group: Pre-Formation

In Sitio Marang, a coastal barangay in Tawi-Tawi, twelve women dry seaweed on bamboo racks every afternoon. They sell to the same middleman. They earn the same low price. They have talked for months about pooling their harvest and selling directly to buyers in Zamboanga. They have the idea. They have the people. What they do not have is a roadmap.

This chapter is that roadmap.

Before you file a single document, before you draft your Articles of Cooperation, before you reserve a name with CSEA – you must build the foundation. That foundation has four parts: confirming your common bond, assembling your organizing group, conducting an economic survey, and completing the Pre-Registration Seminar. Skip any one of these, and your registration will fail. Do them well, and you will walk into CSEA’s office with a cooperative that is ready – not just on paper, but in practice.

3.1 Is Your Community Ready for a Cooperative?

A cooperative begins long before anyone fills out a form. It begins when a group of people share a problem and believe they can solve it together. But belief alone is not enough. The law requires a specific foundation. Your community must meet it.

3.1.1 The Common Bond of Interest Requirement

The common bond of interest is the legal thread that ties your members together. Without it, CSEA will not register your cooperative. Section 5 of BTA Bill No. 210 states the requirement directly: organizers must be “natural persons who are Filipino citizens, of legal age, having a common bond of interest and are actually residing or working in the intended area of operation.”1

The common bond can take several forms:

Type of Common Bond What It Means Bangsamoro Example
Geographic (Residential) Members live in the same barangay, municipality, or province Fisherfolk in Bongao forming a marketing cooperative
Occupational Members share the same trade or profession Seaweed farmers across three barangays in Tawi-Tawi
Institutional Members work for the same employer or agency BARMM ministry employees forming a credit cooperative
Associational Members belong to the same organization Mosque congregation forming a consumer cooperative

Choose the common bond that most accurately describes your group. Do not force a bond that does not exist. If your members are seaweed farmers from five different barangays, your bond is occupational, not geographic. If your members are neighbors who share no livelihood, your bond is residential. The bond you declare must match reality – CSEA verifies this during the evaluation process.

For our Sitio Marang group, the common bond is clear: occupational. All twelve women (and at least three more they plan to recruit) are seaweed farmers. They live in the same barangay, which gives them a geographic bond as well. But the occupational bond is stronger because it connects directly to the cooperative’s planned business activity.

Why the common bond matters beyond compliance. The common bond is not just a legal box to check. It determines whether your members will actually patronize the cooperative. A cooperative of seaweed farmers will naturally buy drying supplies, sell harvests, and share equipment through the cooperative. A cooperative where the only bond is living near each other may struggle to find services that all members need. The stronger the bond, the higher the patronage. The higher the patronage, the stronger the cooperative.

3.1.2 Assessing Community Readiness

Before you invest weeks of organizing work, run a quick assessment. Not every group with a common bond is ready for a cooperative. Readiness depends on four conditions:

1. Economic viability. Is there a real economic problem that a cooperative can solve? The women in Sitio Marang sell their seaweed to a single middleman at PHP 35 per kilo of dried seaweed. Direct buyers in Zamboanga pay PHP 55-65 per kilo. That price gap is the economic case for a cooperative. If no price gap, no inefficiency, and no unmet need exists, a cooperative may not be the right vehicle.

2. Willingness to commit capital. Every member must contribute share capital. For a primary cooperative, the minimum paid-up capital is PHP 15,000 across all members.2 That means if you have 15 members, each member needs to contribute at least PHP 1,000 to meet the floor. Can your prospective members afford this? If the answer is uncertain, do not abandon the idea – but plan for how members will build their contributions over time.

3. Leadership availability. A cooperative needs leaders who can manage meetings, keep records, handle money, and represent the group to CSEA and other agencies. Look at your organizing group. Who can serve as interim chairperson? Who is good with numbers? Who can write reports? If all the leadership falls on one person, you have a problem. Cooperatives run on shared governance, not one-person management.

4. Existing informal cooperation. Groups that already cooperate informally – sharing boats, pooling harvests, lending each other supplies – make the strongest cooperative foundations. They already trust each other. They already understand collective action. The cooperative simply formalizes what they are already doing.

Community Readiness Checklist

Use this checklist before proceeding to the organizing stage. Mark each item honestly.

# Readiness Indicator Yes/No Notes
1 We have at least 15 people interested in forming a cooperative
2 All prospective members are Filipino citizens of legal age (18+)
3 We share a clear common bond (geographic, occupational, institutional, or associational)
4 We can identify a specific economic problem the cooperative will solve
5 Members are willing to contribute share capital (minimum PHP 1,000 each for 15 members)
6 At least 3-5 members can take on leadership roles
7 No single person dominates all decision-making
8 We already cooperate informally (shared resources, group buying, etc.)
9 We can identify products or services the cooperative will provide
10 We are willing to attend the Pre-Registration Seminar (3 hours 45 minutes)

Scoring. If you answered “Yes” to 8-10 items, your group is ready to proceed. If you answered “Yes” to 5-7, you need to strengthen the weaker areas first. Below 5, spend more time on social preparation before organizing.

For our Sitio Marang group: they have 12 members (need 3 more), they share an occupational bond, the price gap creates economic viability, and they already cooperate by sharing drying racks. Their main gap is leadership – only Hadja Nuriya and Bai Asmina have experience managing group activities. They need to identify at least one more leader before moving forward.

3.2 Building the Organizing Group

3.2.1 The 15-Member Minimum and What It Really Takes

The law is clear: at least 15 natural persons are required to organize a primary cooperative.3 All must be Filipino citizens, of legal age, and must share a common bond of interest.

But 15 is the legal minimum, not the practical target.

Aim for 20-30 founding members. Here is why. Some members will lose interest during the organizing process. Others will fail to attend the Pre-Registration Seminar. A few may be unable to contribute their share capital on time. If you start with exactly 15 and lose even one, you fall below the legal threshold and cannot register. Start with a buffer.

The ideal founding membership also depends on your cooperative type and planned activities:

Cooperative Type Minimum Members Recommended Founding Members Why
Credit 15 25-30 More members = larger lending pool
Producer 15 20-25 Collective volume drives better prices
Marketing 15 20-30 More products to aggregate and sell
Multipurpose 15 + additional requirements 30-50 Multiple services require broader base
Consumer 15 25-35 Purchasing power increases with members

Every founding member must complete the PRS. This is not optional. Under BTA Bill No. 210, Section 5, “a prospective member of a primary cooperative must have completed a Pre-registration Seminar.”4 If even one of your 15 members lacks a PRS certificate, CSEA will reject the application.

For our Sitio Marang group: They have 12 women. They need at least 3 more. Hadja Nuriya knows five other seaweed farmers in the adjacent sitio who have expressed interest. Recruit them. Aim for 18-20 founding members so the group can absorb any dropouts during the organizing process.

3.2.2 Identifying Leaders and Assigning Pre-Formation Roles

Before you hold your first formal organizing meeting, identify who will lead the pre-formation process. You are not yet electing official officers – that happens after registration, at the organizational meeting.5 But you need an interim organizing committee to drive the formation forward.

The three essential pre-formation roles:

1. Interim Chairperson. This person leads the organizing meetings, keeps the group focused, and serves as the point of contact with CSEA. Look for someone who is respected by the group, can facilitate discussions without dominating them, and has enough time to attend multiple meetings and government offices.

2. Interim Secretary. This person records the minutes of organizing meetings, maintains the list of prospective members, and manages the paperwork. The secretary will eventually prepare (or coordinate the preparation of) the Articles of Cooperation, bylaws, and economic survey. Look for someone who is literate, organized, and comfortable with documentation.

3. Interim Treasurer. This person collects and safeguards initial capital contributions from members. The treasurer will eventually execute the Treasurer’s Affidavit – a notarized document certifying that at least 25% of authorized capital has been subscribed and at least 25% of subscriptions have been paid.6 Look for someone trustworthy, numerate, and willing to be bonded.

Warning: Do not let one person hold all three roles. This defeats the democratic principle of cooperative governance. Even at the pre-formation stage, distribute responsibility. If your group only has one person willing to lead, you are not ready to form a cooperative.

Additional Pre-Formation Roles

For larger organizing groups, add these roles:

Role Responsibility
Membership Committee Lead Recruits new members, verifies eligibility, maintains the roster
PRS Coordinator Contacts CSEA to schedule the PRS, ensures all members attend
Capital Campaign Lead Tracks share capital commitments and payments from each member
Economic Survey Lead Gathers the data needed for the economic survey (market prices, costs, potential revenue)

For our Sitio Marang group: Hadja Nuriya becomes interim chairperson – she is respected, experienced, and connected with other seaweed farmers. Bai Asmina, who finished two years of college and keeps clean records for the barangay health station, becomes interim secretary. Sahaya, who managed the funds for their mosque’s feeding program for three years, becomes interim treasurer. The three additional women Hadja Nuriya will recruit can take on membership and PRS coordination roles.

3.3 Conducting the Economic Survey

The economic survey is one of the four mandatory documents you must submit to CSEA for registration.7 But it is more than a requirement. It is your cooperative’s first business plan. A well-prepared economic survey forces you to answer the hard questions before you commit capital and effort.

3.3.1 Area of Operation, Size of Membership, and Viability Data

BTA Bill No. 210, Section 6 requires every group intending to form a cooperative to submit “an economic survey, indicating therein the area of operation, the size of membership, and other pertinent data in a format provided by the Authority.”8 The same section mandates a “general statement describing, among others the structure and purposes of the proposed cooperative” and requires that “the structure and actual staffing pattern of the cooperative shall include a bookkeeper.”

The economic survey must demonstrate four things:

1. Demand for cooperative services. Who will use the cooperative’s products or services? How often? How much will they spend? For a seaweed marketing cooperative, this means documenting how many kilos of dried seaweed your members produce, how often they harvest, and what buyers are willing to pay.

2. A viable member base. How many people can you realistically recruit? What is their economic profile? Can they afford the share capital? Will they patronize the cooperative regularly?

3. Available resources. What physical, financial, and human resources does the group already have? Drying racks? Storage facilities? A bookkeeper? A truck? What do you still need?

4. Economic feasibility. Based on projected revenue and expenses, can the cooperative sustain itself? Will it generate enough income to cover costs, build reserves, and provide benefits to members?

The CDA’s official economic survey form organizes this data into four major sections:9

Section What It Covers
I. General Information Type of cooperative, address, area of operation, common bond, membership composition, capital structure
II-A. Economic Aspect Existing competitors, member support strategies, transaction scope, planned business activities
II-B. Financial Aspect Capital sources, initial operating capital, projected revenue and expenses, investment plans
II-C. Technical Aspect Equipment and facilities needed, procurement plans, required skills and training
II-D. Organizational Structure Board qualifications, staffing plan, education programs, committee members

3.3.2 The Economic Survey Template (Step by Step)

Walk through each section of the economic survey using the Sitio Marang seaweed cooperative as an example. The official CDA/CSEA template is the authoritative form – what follows is a guide for filling it out correctly.10

Background Section

Write a brief narrative about your community and why a cooperative is needed. Keep it to one paragraph.

Filled-in example:

Sitio Marang is a coastal community of approximately 200 households in Barangay Simunul, Municipality of Simunul, Tawi-Tawi. The primary livelihood is seaweed farming, with approximately 45 households engaged in cultivating, drying, and selling Kappaphycus alvarezii (cottonii). Currently, individual farmers sell dried seaweed to a single middleman at PHP 35 per kilo – well below the PHP 55-65 per kilo offered by direct buyers in Zamboanga City. The lack of organized marketing, limited drying facilities, and absence of collective bargaining power have kept farm-gate prices low. This cooperative aims to aggregate member harvests, improve drying quality, and sell directly to processors and exporters.

Rationale Section

Explain why the cooperative form is the best solution. Why not an association? Why not a sole proprietorship?

Filled-in example:

Individual farmers lack the volume, storage capacity, and market connections to negotiate directly with processors. A cooperative enables members to pool harvests (achieving the 5-ton minimum required by most direct buyers), share the cost of drying and storage facilities, and negotiate collectively for better prices. The cooperative structure also allows access to CSEA technical assistance, government livelihood programs, and the Education and Training Fund mandated by law.

Section I: General Information

Field What to Write Sitio Marang Example
Type of Cooperative Check one: Credit, Producer, Marketing, MPC, etc. Marketing
Office Address Full address: No., Street, Barangay, Municipality, Province Purok 3, Sitio Marang, Barangay Simunul, Simunul, Tawi-Tawi
Area of Operation Check one: Barangay, Municipal, Provincial, Regional, National Municipal
Common Bond Check one: Residential, Institutional, Occupational, Associational Occupational
Composition of Members Check all that apply: Farmers, Fisherfolks, Women, etc. Fisherfolks, Women
No. of Founding Members Number of organizers 18
Projected Membership Growth Year 1, Year 2, Year 3 Year 1: 25; Year 2: 35; Year 3: 50

Capital Structure:

Item Computation Sitio Marang Example
Authorized Capital Set by organizers PHP 100,000.00
Subscribed Capital Must be at least 25% of Authorized PHP 36,000.00 (36%)
Paid-up Capital Must be at least 25% of Subscribed AND at least PHP 15,000 PHP 18,000.00 (50% of subscribed)
Par Value per Share Set by organizers PHP 500.00 per share

How the capital computation works. BTA Bill No. 210, Section 9(d) requires that at least 25% of authorized share capital be subscribed and at least 25% of subscriptions be paid up, with a floor of PHP 15,000.11 For 18 members contributing PHP 1,000 each in initial paid-up capital, total paid-up is PHP 18,000 – above the PHP 15,000 floor. Each member holds 2 shares at PHP 500 par value.

Section II-A: Economic Aspect

Existing cooperatives in the area. Research whether any cooperative in your area already offers the same services. If yes, name them. This is not a disqualifier – but CSEA wants to know you are aware of the competitive landscape.

Strategies for member support. Check all that apply from the template: collective purchases, commitment on lending policies, active participation in cooperative affairs, or specify others. For a marketing cooperative, add “collective marketing of member harvests” and “shared access to drying and storage facilities.”

Transaction scope. Will you transact with members only, or with members and non-members? Most cooperatives choose members and non-members – this allows the cooperative to aggregate volume from non-member farmers (who pay a service fee) while building the case for those farmers to join as members.

Three-year business activity plan:

Year Planned Activities
Year 1 Aggregate and market member seaweed harvests; establish quality grading system; acquire basic drying equipment
Year 2 Expand to include seaweed nursery services; establish direct buyer contracts in Zamboanga; add 10 new members
Year 3 Begin semi-processed carrageenan production; explore export markets; expand to neighboring barangays

Section II-B: Financial Aspect

Capital sources. Check all that apply: share capital subscription, revolving capital, loans, donations/subsidies/grants, fundraising. For most new cooperatives, the primary source is share capital subscription. Grants from CSEA programs like the PESO Project (Profitability and Equity Support Oriented) may become available after registration.12

Three-year financial projection:

Item Year 1 Year 2 Year 3
Projected Revenue PHP 275,000 PHP 450,000 PHP 720,000
Estimated Expenses PHP 210,000 PHP 340,000 PHP 510,000
Projected Net Surplus PHP 65,000 PHP 110,000 PHP 210,000
Initial Operating Capital PHP 18,000 – –

How the Sitio Marang numbers work. Year 1: 18 members producing an average of 300 kg dried seaweed each per year = 5,400 kg total. Sold at PHP 50/kg (conservative estimate vs. PHP 55-65 direct buyer price) = PHP 270,000 in sales revenue. Add PHP 5,000 from service fees charged to non-member sellers. Expenses include transportation to Zamboanga (PHP 90,000), drying supplies (PHP 40,000), packaging (PHP 25,000), administrative costs (PHP 30,000), and equipment maintenance (PHP 25,000).

Section II-C: Technical Aspect

List the equipment and facilities your cooperative needs. The template provides checkboxes for common items. For a seaweed marketing cooperative:

Equipment/Facility Status Estimated Cost
Solar dryers (improved type) To be acquired PHP 45,000
Weighing scale (digital, 100kg capacity) To be acquired PHP 8,000
Storage area/warehouse Existing (member’s structure) PHP 0 (donated use)
Tables and chairs for office To be acquired PHP 5,000
Calculator Existing PHP 0
Filing cabinet To be acquired PHP 3,000
Packaging materials To be purchased quarterly PHP 25,000/year

Procurement mode. For initial equipment: cash purchase from paid-up capital. For larger items (solar dryers): explore grant programs through CSEA, MAFAR, or the 3ZERO Initiative partnership between CSEA, MAFAR, WFP, and ACTED.13

Required skills and training. List the training your members need beyond the PRS: quality grading of dried seaweed, basic bookkeeping, cooperative governance, and marketing skills.

Section II-D: Organizational Structure

Describe the qualifications your Board of Directors should possess. Name the officers and employees the cooperative will hire for initial operations.

Staffing plan for a small cooperative:

Position Name Status Compensation
Manager Hadja Nuriya (interim) Volunteer None (first year)
Bookkeeper To be hired Part-time/Contractual PHP 3,000/month
Collector/Marketing Officer Bai Asmina Part-time/Volunteer Honorarium PHP 1,500/month

BTA Bill No. 210, Section 6 requires that the staffing pattern include a bookkeeper.14 This is not optional. Even if your cooperative is small, you must have a bookkeeper. For the first year, this can be a part-time contractual hire rather than a full-time employee.

Certification. The founding Board of Directors must sign and certify the economic survey. It must then be notarized.15

3.4 The Pre-Registration Seminar (PRS)

The Pre-Registration Seminar is a mandatory training that every prospective cooperative member must complete before registration. No PRS certificate, no registration. There are no exceptions.

3.4.1 PRS Content Requirements Under Law

RA 9520 (Philippine Cooperative Code of 2008) established the PRS requirement for all cooperatives nationwide.16 CDA Memorandum Circular No. 2025-11 – the Revised Guidelines for Conducting Pre-Registration Seminars for Prospective Cooperatives – sets the current national standards.17 In BARMM, the Cooperative and Social Enterprise Authority (CSEA) conducts the PRS, applying these standards within the Bangsamoro context.18

The PRS runs for 3 hours and 45 minutes and covers five major topics:19

Module Content Duration
1. Preliminaries Opening program, introduction of participants 10 minutes
2. Objectives and Description of PRS Why PRS exists, what participants will learn 20 minutes
3. The CDA/CSEA and Its Mandate Salient provisions of the CDA Charter (RA 11364), CSEA’s role in BARMM 15 minutes
4. Introduction to Cooperatives Cooperative identity, types and services, purpose, member rights and responsibilities 2 hours
5. Requirements and Process of Registration Documentary requirements, E-CoopRIS online system, what to expect after registration 1 hour
Total 3 hours 45 minutes

Module 4 is the heart of the PRS. In two hours, participants learn what a cooperative truly is – not just a business, but a values-driven organization built on seven internationally recognized principles.20 They learn the difference between a cooperative and a corporation. They learn their rights as members (to vote, to inspect books, to receive patronage refunds) and their responsibilities (to patronize, to attend meetings, to pay capital contributions).

What the PRS does not cover. The PRS is an orientation, not a comprehensive training. It does not teach bookkeeping, business planning, or management. Those come later through the Basic Cooperative Course (8-16 hours for new members) and Officer Training programs (24-40 hours for directors and committee members).33 The PRS gives every prospective member enough knowledge to make an informed decision: do I want to join this cooperative?

Why the PRS matters beyond compliance. Many organizing groups treat the PRS as a box to check. That is a mistake. The PRS is where your members first learn the seven cooperative principles – the values that distinguish a cooperative from a business. Members who understand these principles make better decisions in General Assembly meetings. They hold their Board of Directors accountable. They patronize the cooperative because they understand how patronage refunds work. Treat the PRS as the first investment in your cooperative’s human capital, not just a registration hurdle.

3.4.2 Finding an Accredited PRS Provider

In BARMM, the PRS is conducted by CSEA, not CDA. Contact CSEA directly to schedule a session for your organizing group.

CSEA Office: JICA Building, BGC Gov. Gutierrez Avenue Brgy. RH XII, Cotabato City Maguindanao 9600

Contact: (00) 552-110521

How to request a PRS session:

  1. Contact CSEA and ask about the PRS schedule. CSEA posts schedules on their official social media platforms.
  2. Request a group session. MC 2025-11 encourages prospective members to attend as a group.22 This is practical – your entire organizing group completes the requirement at the same time.
  3. Coordinate with your LGU. Your municipal or city government, through the Cooperative Development Officer (CDO), can help coordinate with CSEA. The CDO can provide the venue, meals, and logistical support for face-to-face sessions.23 If your LGU does not have a CDO (many in BARMM do not), contact CSEA directly.
  4. Choose your delivery mode. PRS sessions can be conducted online or face-to-face.24 For remote island communities in BaSulTa (Basilan, Tawi-Tawi), online delivery may be more accessible – but ensure all participants have internet access.

Cost: Free. The PRS is conducted free of charge. No fees shall be collected from participants.25 For face-to-face sessions, participants cover their own meals. If your LGU provides the venue and meals, participants pay nothing at all.

Special schedule requests. If the regular PRS schedule does not work for your group, you may request a special session. This requires prior authorization from the CSEA Regional Director and depends on staff availability.26

3.4.3 What Happens If Members Skip the PRS

The consequence is absolute: your cooperative will not be registered.

The PRS Certificate is one of the mandatory documentary requirements for registration.27 CSEA will not process your application without a valid PRS certificate for every founding member listed in the Articles of Cooperation. There is no workaround.

The PRS certificate is valid for three years from the date of issuance.28 This means your organizing group has three years from the date they complete the PRS to finalize their registration documents and submit to CSEA. If the certificate expires before you register, every member must retake the PRS.

Practical tip: Schedule the PRS early in your organizing process. Do not wait until all documents are ready. Complete the PRS while you are still drafting the economic survey and Articles of Cooperation. This serves two purposes: (1) it gets the legal requirement out of the way, and (2) the PRS content helps your members understand cooperative principles, which improves the quality of your founding documents.

What if a member joins after the PRS? If you recruit additional members between the PRS and registration, those new members must also complete a PRS. They can attend a separate session – they do not need to attend the same session as the original group.

For our Sitio Marang group: Hadja Nuriya contacts CSEA in Cotabato City and learns that a PRS session is scheduled in two weeks. She coordinates with the barangay captain for a venue. The LGU agrees to provide the space and snacks. All 18 prospective members attend the 3-hour-45-minute session. They receive their PRS certificates. The certificates are valid until 2029. The clock is ticking – but they have plenty of time.

3.5 Drafting Your Initial Business Concept

Before you write the Articles of Cooperation, you need clarity on what your cooperative will actually do. The economic survey captures financial projections and operational data. But first, you need a business concept – a clear picture of the problem you solve, for whom, and how.

3.5.1 The Lean Canvas for Cooperatives (Adapted)

The Lean Canvas is a one-page business modeling tool created by Ash Maurya.29 It forces you to think through nine critical elements of your business. Originally designed for startups, it adapts powerfully to cooperatives – with one key difference. Where a startup’s “unfair advantage” might be proprietary technology, a cooperative’s unfair advantage is its community bond and collective action.

Here are the nine blocks of the Lean Canvas, adapted for cooperative context, with a filled-in example for the Sitio Marang seaweed cooperative:

Block 1: Problem

What problem does your cooperative solve? List the top three problems your members face.

# Problem Current Alternative
1 Low farm-gate prices (PHP 35/kg vs. PHP 55-65/kg direct) Sell individually to a single middleman
2 Poor drying quality reduces grade and price Use makeshift bamboo racks exposed to rain
3 No access to market information or direct buyers Rely on middleman’s price, no negotiation power

Always start with the problem. A cooperative that solves a real, painful problem will attract and retain members. A cooperative formed without a clear problem to solve will struggle from day one.

Block 2: Customer Segments

Who benefits from your cooperative? For a cooperative, your “customers” are two groups: your members (who are also your owners) and your external buyers (who purchase what your cooperative sells).

Segment Who They Are
Members (primary) 18 women seaweed farmers in Simunul, Tawi-Tawi
External buyers Seaweed processors and exporters in Zamboanga City; carrageenan factories
Future members Estimated 30+ additional seaweed farmers in adjacent barangays

Block 3: Unique Value Proposition

What makes your cooperative’s offer compelling? Write one sentence that captures your cooperative’s unique value.

“Aggregated, quality-graded dried seaweed from Tawi-Tawi’s top-producing coastal communities – delivered in bulk directly to processors at competitive prices.”

For members, the value is simple: higher income per kilo of seaweed. For buyers, the value is reliable volume and consistent quality – something individual farmers cannot guarantee.

Block 4: Solution

How does your cooperative solve the problem? Map each problem to a specific solution.

Problem Solution
Low farm-gate prices Aggregate harvests and sell directly to processors, eliminating the middleman
Poor drying quality Invest in improved solar dryers; train members in quality grading standards
No market access Build direct buyer relationships in Zamboanga; join CSEA trade fairs and the 3ZERO Initiative

Block 5: Channels

How will you reach your members and buyers?

Channel Purpose
Barangay meetings and mosque announcements Recruit and communicate with members
CSEA trade fairs and product expos Connect with buyers
Direct outreach to Zamboanga processors Establish buying contracts
Mobile phone and social media groups Coordinate harvests and logistics

Block 6: Revenue Streams

How does the cooperative earn income?

Revenue Source Description Projected Annual Amount
Marketing margin Spread between member farm-gate price and buyer price PHP 240,000 (Year 1)
Service fees Fees charged to non-member farmers who use cooperative marketing services PHP 15,000
Drying facility rental Fees for use of improved solar dryers PHP 20,000

Block 7: Cost Structure

What are your major costs?

Cost Category Annual Estimate
Transportation (to Zamboanga) PHP 90,000
Drying supplies and equipment maintenance PHP 40,000
Packaging materials PHP 25,000
Administrative (bookkeeper, communications, office supplies) PHP 40,000
Surety bond and registration fees PHP 15,000 (Year 1 only)

Block 8: Key Metrics

What numbers tell you whether the cooperative is working?

Metric Target (Year 1)
Total volume marketed (kg) 5,400 kg
Average selling price per kg PHP 50+
Member patronage rate 80%+ of members selling through the cooperative
Net surplus PHP 65,000+
Membership growth From 18 to 25

Block 9: Unfair Advantage

What do you have that cannot be easily copied? This is where cooperatives shine. Your unfair advantage is not a patent or proprietary algorithm. It is your community.

Tightly-knit community of women seaweed farmers who have worked together for years, sharing knowledge, labor, and drying facilities. This trust and social cohesion cannot be replicated by a competing trader or corporation.

How to Use the Lean Canvas in Practice

Step 1: Gather your organizing committee. Set aside two hours. Bring a large sheet of paper or use a whiteboard. Draw the nine-block grid.

Step 2: Fill in the Problem block first. Do not start with the solution. If your group cannot agree on the top three problems, you are not ready to form a cooperative. Spend as long as needed on this block.

Step 3: Fill in Customer Segments second. Who exactly are the members? Who are the buyers? Be specific. “Women seaweed farmers in Simunul aged 25-55” is better than “farmers.”

Step 4: Work through the remaining blocks. There is no strict order for blocks 3-9, but most groups find it natural to move from Unique Value Proposition to Solution to Channels to Revenue.

Step 5: Test your canvas against reality. After completing the canvas, ask three hard questions: (1) Is the problem painful enough that members will pay capital and patronize the cooperative? (2) Can we actually deliver the solution with our current resources? (3) Do the revenue numbers cover the cost numbers? If the answer to any of these is “no,” revise the canvas before proceeding.

The Lean Canvas is not a registration requirement. CSEA does not ask for it. But filling it out before you write the economic survey sharpens your thinking. Every number in the economic survey comes from the analysis you do here. The Lean Canvas asks the “why” and “what.” The economic survey asks the “how much” and “when.”

3.5.2 Identifying Your Products, Services, and Target Market

Your cooperative must offer specific products or services. Vague statements like “general trading” or “help our members” will not survive the economic survey, and they will not attract patronage. Define what your cooperative does in concrete terms.

Start with what your members already produce or need. The strongest cooperatives build on existing livelihoods. Do not form a coffee cooperative in a community that grows seaweed. Match the cooperative to the community.

BARMM’s top commodities offer proven opportunities for cooperatives:30

Commodity BARMM Share of National Production Cooperative Opportunity
Seaweed 48.42% (#1 nationally) Marketing, drying, semi-processing
Cassava 41.70% (#1 nationally) Processing (flour, chips), marketing
Rubber Significant producer Marketing, processing
Coffee Growing sector Quality control, direct trade
Coconut Major livelihood crop Copra marketing, oil processing
Rice Staple crop Production, milling, consumer
Fisheries Abundant marine resources Marketing, cold storage, value-adding

Conduct basic market analysis. Answer four questions:

1. Who will buy your product or service? Name specific buyers, not categories. “Zamboanga seaweed processors” is better than “the market.” Visit the buyers if possible. Ask what volume they need, what quality standards they require, and what price they offer.

2. What volume can your members produce? Be conservative. Calculate based on current production, not aspirational targets. If your 18 members each produce 300 kg of dried seaweed per year, your Year 1 volume is 5,400 kg – not 10,000 kg.

3. What is the price gap? Compare the price your members currently receive (selling individually) with the price the cooperative can negotiate (selling in bulk, directly). If there is no meaningful price gap, the cooperative may not add enough value to justify the effort.

4. Who is the competition? Are there middlemen, traders, or other cooperatives serving the same market? What advantages do they have? What can your cooperative offer that they cannot?

Document your market analysis in the economic survey. The data you gather here fills in Sections II-A (Economic Aspect) and II-B (Financial Aspect) of the CDA/CSEA economic survey template.

A note on Shari’ah compliance. If your cooperative will operate under Islamic principles – and most cooperatives in BARMM should consider this – your products, services, and business activities must comply with Shari’ah. Under BTA Bill No. 210, Section 7, this means your cooperative must fulfill the objectives of Shari’ah (maqasid al-Shari’ah): preservation of faith, life, lineage, intellect, and wealth.31 Practically, this means no interest-based lending (riba), no gambling (maysir), no speculative transactions (gharar), and no dealing in haram products.32 For a seaweed marketing cooperative, Shari’ah compliance is straightforward – seaweed is halal, and marketing does not involve interest. For a credit cooperative, you will need Shari’ah-compliant alternatives like Murabaha (cost-plus financing) or Musharakah (partnership). Chapter 7 covers Shari’ah-compliant financial operations in detail.

Product and Service Definition Template

Use this template to clearly define what your cooperative will offer:

# Product or Service Target Customer Pricing Strategy Estimated Annual Revenue
1 Aggregated dried seaweed (bulk) Zamboanga processors Market price minus 5% handling PHP 240,000
2 Improved solar drying service Members and non-members PHP 2/kg drying fee for non-members; free for members PHP 20,000
3 Seaweed nursery seedlings Farmers in adjacent barangays (Year 2) PHP 15/kg of seedling PHP 25,000

Pulling It All Together: The Pre-Formation Checklist

Before moving to Chapter 4 (Registration), verify that your organizing group has completed every pre-formation requirement. Use this checklist:

Step Task Status Required For
1 Confirmed common bond of interest ☐ Registration
2 Assembled 15+ founding members (recommend 20+) ☐ Registration
3 All founding members are Filipino citizens, 18+ ☐ Registration
4 Assigned interim organizing committee (Chair, Secretary, Treasurer) ☐ Internal organization
5 Completed the economic survey ☐ Registration
6 All founding members completed the PRS ☐ Registration
7 PRS certificates are valid (within 3 years of issuance) ☐ Registration
8 Drafted initial business concept (Lean Canvas or equivalent) ☐ Economic survey input
9 Identified products/services and target market ☐ Economic survey input
10 Capital commitments collected from members (minimum PHP 15,000 total paid-up) ☐ Registration
11 Bookkeeper identified or hired ☐ Registration (per BTA Bill 210, Sec. 6)

If all boxes are checked, you are ready for the next phase: drafting the Articles of Cooperation and Bylaws, reserving your cooperative name, and submitting your registration package to CSEA. That is Chapter 4.

The Sitio Marang women are ready. They have 18 members with a clear occupational bond. Their economic survey shows a viable seaweed marketing business. They have completed the PRS. They know their products, their buyers, and their numbers. The idea that started over bamboo drying racks is now an organizing group with a plan.

Your community can do the same. Start with the common bond. Build the group. Do the research. Complete the seminar. Then walk into CSEA’s office with confidence.

Footnotes

  1. BTA Bill No. 210 (Bangsamoro Cooperative and Social Enterprise Code), Book II, Chapter I, Section 5. ↩

  2. BTA Bill No. 210, Section 9(d); CDA Memorandum Circular No. 2016-02, amending MC 2015-01. The minimum paid-up capital for a primary cooperative is PHP 15,000. ↩

  3. BTA Bill No. 210, Section 5: “Fifteen (15) or more natural persons who are Filipino citizens, of legal age, having a common bond of interest and are actually residing or working in the intended area of operation, may organize a primary cooperative.” ↩

  4. BTA Bill No. 210, Section 5. ↩

  5. CDA MC 2015-01 requires the organizational meeting to be conducted within 30 days of registration, where the first Board of Directors and officers are formally elected. ↩

  6. BTA Bill No. 210, Section 9(d). The Treasurer’s Affidavit must be notarized and must certify that at least 25% of authorized share capital has been subscribed and at least 25% of total subscriptions have been paid, with a minimum floor of PHP 15,000. ↩

  7. CDA MC 2015-01, as amended by MC 2016-02, lists the economic survey as one of eight required registration documents. See also BTA Bill No. 210, Section 6. ↩

  8. BTA Bill No. 210, Section 6. ↩

  9. CDA Official Economic Survey Form, prescribed pursuant to RA 9520 (Philippine Cooperative Code of 2008). Available at: https://cda.gov.ph/downloads/cooperative-pro-forma-registration-documents/ ↩

  10. The CDA Economic Survey template is the standard form used by both CDA (nationwide) and CSEA (BARMM). Download the official form from CDA’s website or request a copy from CSEA. ↩

  11. BTA Bill No. 210, Section 9(d). ↩

  12. Bangsamoro Development Plan (BDP) 2023-2028, Chapter 7. The PESO (Profitability and Equity Support Oriented) Project provides financial and technical assistance to improve cooperative profitability. ↩

  13. The 3ZERO Initiative is a partnership between CSEA, MAFAR, the World Food Programme (WFP), and ACTED, focused on sustainable and inclusive agriculture. Source: CSEA official programs. ↩

  14. BTA Bill No. 210, Section 6: “the structure and actual staffing pattern of the cooperative shall include a bookkeeper.” ↩

  15. CDA Official Economic Survey Form, Certification section. The economic survey must be signed by the founding Board of Directors and notarized. ↩

  16. Republic Act No. 9520, Philippine Cooperative Code of 2008. The PRS requirement is operationalized through CDA memorandum circulars governing registration. ↩

  17. CDA Memorandum Circular No. 2025-11, “Revised Guidelines for Conducting Pre-Registration Seminars for Prospective Cooperatives,” approved by CDA BOD Resolution No. 291, S-2025, issued September 25, 2025. Signed by USEC. Alexander B. Raquepo, Chairman. ↩

  18. In BARMM, the Cooperative and Social Enterprise Authority (CSEA) exercises the functions of CDA, including the conduct of Pre-Registration Seminars. Source: BDP 2023-2028, Chapter 7; CSEA mandate. ↩

  19. CDA MC 2025-11, Section VII (PRS Course Outline). ↩

  20. The seven internationally recognized cooperative principles are established by the International Cooperative Alliance (ICA): Voluntary and Open Membership; Democratic Member Control; Member Economic Participation; Autonomy and Independence; Education, Training, and Information; Cooperation Among Cooperatives; Concern for Community. These are codified in BTA Bill No. 210, Section 5. ↩

  21. CSEA Office, JICA Building, BGC, Gov. Gutierrez Avenue, Brgy. RH XII, Cotabato City, Maguindanao 9600. Phone: (00) 552-1105. ↩

  22. CDA MC 2025-11, Section V.7: “It is encouraged that the required number of prospective members for cooperative registration attend the PRS as a group.” ↩

  23. CDA MC 2025-11, Section VI.2. LGUs through their Cooperative Development Officers (CDOs) may provide venue, meals, and facilities; coordinate with CDA/CSEA; conduct orientation on cooperative development programs; and provide logistical support for online PRS sessions. See also RA 11535, Article XVII, Sec. 487, 3.ii. ↩

  24. CDA MC 2025-11, Section V.8. ↩

  25. CDA MC 2025-11, Section V.7: “The Cooperative Development Authority (CDA) shall conduct Pre-Registration Seminars (PRS) free of charge. No fees shall be collected from participants.” ↩

  26. CDA MC 2025-11, Section V.5: Special schedule requests require “prior authorization from the Regional Director.” ↩

  27. CDA MC 2015-01, Documentary Requirements. The PRS Certificate is one of eight required documents for registration. See also BTA Bill No. 210, Section 5. ↩

  28. CDA MC 2025-11, Section V.10: “A Certificate of Completion shall be valid for a period of three (3) years from the date of issuance.” ↩

  29. Ash Maurya, Running Lean: Iterate from Plan A to a Plan That Works (O’Reilly Media, 2012). The Lean Canvas is adapted from Alexander Osterwalder’s Business Model Canvas with a focus on problem-solution fit. ↩

  30. Bangsamoro Development Plan (BDP) 2023-2028, Chapter 7. BARMM is the #1 national producer of seaweed (48.42% of national production) and cassava (41.70% of national production). Other priority commodities include rubber, coffee, mangosteen, and lanzones. ↩

  31. BTA Bill No. 210, Section 7(a). ↩

  32. BTA Bill No. 210, Section 7. See also the Shari’ah Compliance section in Chapter 7 of this guidebook. ↩

  33. Cooperative training levels are structured in a four-tier framework: PRS (3-4 hours), Basic Course (8-16 hours), Intermediate/Officer Training (24-40 hours), and Advanced/Leadership (40+ hours). See Chapter 10 of this guidebook for the full training framework. ↩

Back to Table of Contents
04
Registration: From Application to Certificate

Chapter 4 – Registration: From Application to Certificate

Registration is where your cooperative becomes real. Before this moment, you have an idea, an organizing group, a completed economic survey, and a stack of PRS certificates. After this moment, you have a legal entity – one that can open bank accounts, enter contracts, receive grants, and transact business in its own name. Every step in Chapters 2 and 3 was preparation. This chapter is execution.

The registration process has a reputation for being difficult. It is not difficult. It is detailed. There is a difference. A difficult process would require special expertise or connections. This process requires completeness, accuracy, and patience. You will prepare two core documents – the Articles of Cooperation (AOC) and the Bylaws – assemble supporting requirements, file them with CSEA, and wait for your Certificate of Registration (COR). Every one of these steps has clear rules. Follow them, and you will be registered.

This chapter walks you through each step. By the end, you will know exactly what to prepare, exactly how to prepare it, and exactly what to expect once you file.

4.1 Overview: The Registration Process

4.1.1 Timeline: What to Expect from Filing to COR

The registration process runs across three phases. Here is the full sequence from your first organizing meeting through COR issuance:

Phase 1: Document Preparation (4-8 weeks)

  1. Reserve your cooperative name with CSEA (1-3 days)
