Level 300 FRM Officers of savings and credit cooperatives (PHP 5M+ deposits) Financial, Risk, and Credit Management 16 hours
Level 300 BLT Officers and managers Business Line Training 4-8 hours
Level 300 BARMM-1 Officers of BARMM cooperatives BDP Alignment and Moral Governance 4-6 hours
Level 400 CLT Chairpersons, senior officers, managers Cooperative Leadership 40+ hours
Level 400 STOCK Former combatant cooperative members Combatant Transitional Training 24-40 hours
Level 400 LAKAS Social enterprise organizers Social Enterprise Development 16-24 hours
Level 400 AGRI Farmer organizations, agri-cooperatives Agri-Cooperative Strengthening 24-40 hours

10.2.1 Level 100 (PRS and Basic): Foundation Knowledge

Level 100 is where every cooperative member begins. This tier has two components: the Pre-Registration Seminar (PRS) for prospective members, and the Fundamentals of Cooperatives Course (FCC) for all members.

Pre-Registration Seminar (PRS). Under CDA MC 2025-11, PRS is a 3-hour 45-minute seminar conducted by the CDA Regional Extension Offices – or, in BARMM, by the Cooperative and Social Enterprise Authority (CSEA).15 PRS is mandatory before registration. Without a PRS Certificate, your cooperative cannot complete the registration process.

The PRS covers five modules:

Module Content Duration
Preliminaries Opening program, participant introductions 10 minutes
Objectives and Description of PRS Why PRS exists, what it covers 20 minutes
The CDA/CSEA and its Mandate Salient provisions of RA 11364, CSEA role in BARMM 15 minutes
Introduction to Cooperatives Cooperative identity, types and services, member rights and duties 2 hours
Requirements and Process of Registration E-CoopRIS, post-registration expectations 1 hour

Key PRS facts your cooperative needs to know:

  • Cost: Free. No fees shall be collected from participants.16
  • Mode: Online or face-to-face.
  • Certificate validity: Three years from date of issuance.17
  • Certificate issuer: In BARMM, the CSEA issues PRS certificates.
  • LGU role: Local Government Units, through their Cooperative Development Officers (CDOs), assist prospective cooperatives with venue, logistics, and coordination with CSEA.18

Fundamentals of Cooperatives Course (FCC). Under CDA MC 2025-09, all cooperatives must ensure that every member completes training on the fundamentals of cooperatives, financial literacy, and leadership and values formation within one year of membership acceptance.19 For existing members at the time the circular took effect, the cooperative must prepare an action plan for compliance within one year.

The FCC curriculum is prescribed by CDA MC 2025-09, Annex A:

Subject Matter Duration
Fundamentals of Cooperatives – Origin and philosophy, statement of cooperative identity, cooperative difference, role in national and international development 6 hours
Cooperative Laws and Relevant Issuances – Salient provisions of RA 9520, RA 11364, RA 10744 6 hours
Leadership and Values Formation 2 hours
Financial Literacy (including digital literacy) 2 hours
Total 16 hours

For BARMM cooperatives, add a continuing education module covering BDP 2023-2028 alignment (Goal 2, Objective 2.5), Moral Governance principles, Shari’ah compliance basics, and CSEA compliance requirements. Budget 4 additional hours for this module. Your Education and Training Committee should integrate this into your annual training plan.

10.2.2 Level 200 (Basic to Intermediate): Governance and Operations

Level 200 is for officers. Every elected and appointed officer must complete required training within the first half of their term.20 This is not optional. Officers who do not complete the training are disqualified from holding office.21

The training requirements differ by cooperative category:

For micro cooperatives (assets below PHP 3 million):

Subject Matter Duration
Cooperative Management and Governance (includes applicable laws and policy formulation) 8 hours
Financial Management (financial statement interpretation, financial ratios, analysis) 8 hours
Line-of-business training (specific to your cooperative’s business) 8 hours
Total 24 hours

For small, medium, and large cooperatives:

Subject Matter Duration
Expanded Cooperative Management and Governance (relevant laws, parliamentary procedure, policy formulation) 16 hours
Financial Management (basic accounting for non-accountants, internal control) 8 hours
Line-of-business training 4 hours
Role/function-specific training (per committee assignment) Minimum 4 hours
Total 32 hours

Authorized providers for Level 200 training:

Provider Conditions
CDA Free of charge (courses 1 and 2 only for micro cooperatives)
Certified Cooperative Development Officers (CDOs) and technical personnel of Local Cooperative Development Offices (LCDO-LGU) Free of charge; must hold Certificate of Authority
Accredited Cooperative Training Providers (CTPros) Fee-based; must hold CDA accreditation
Unions and Federations For their affiliates

Micro cooperatives that graduate to small, medium, or large category must take an additional 8 hours of training on expanded Cooperative Governance and Management, covering international concepts such as the ICA Second Decade Blueprint.22

Continuing education for officers. The law mandates that all cooperatives adopt an annual continuing education program for officers, reflected in the cooperative’s annual development plan.23 Do not treat the initial 24- or 32-hour training as a one-time requirement. Build a yearly training calendar that keeps your officers current on regulatory changes, financial management best practices, and governance standards.

10.2.3 Level 300 (Intermediate): Business Development and Financial Management

Level 300 targets specialized knowledge for cooperatives with significant financial operations. Two triggers bring you to this level.

Trigger 1: Savings and credit operations with PHP 5 million or more in deposit liabilities. If your cooperative is engaged in savings and credit and holds at least PHP 5,000,000.00 in deposit liabilities based on the latest Audited Financial Statement, all officers must complete an additional 16 hours of specialized training:24

Subject Matter Duration
Financial Management (advanced) Included in 16 hours
Basic Accounting for Non-Accountants Included in 16 hours
Internal Control Included in 16 hours
Risk Management Included in 16 hours
Credit Management Included in 16 hours

This training is available free of charge from CDA and certified CDOs/LCDO-LGU technical personnel, or fee-based through accredited CTPros.25

Trigger 2: Credit Surety Fund (CSF) cooperatives. Officers of registered CSF cooperatives must complete a 16-hour startup/refresher course on RA 10744 and its IRR, plus role/function-specific training of at least 4 hours.26

BARMM-specific Level 300 content. For cooperatives operating in the Bangsamoro, add these modules to your Level 300 training plan:

Module Content Duration
BDP 2023-2028 Implementation Strategy 21 (cooperative profitability), Strategy 22 (SE institutionalization), M&E systems 2 hours
Profitability Enhancement PESO Project alignment, financial sustainability metrics, Objective 2.5.1 targets 2 hours
LGU Coordination Working with Municipal Cooperative Officers (MCOs), provincial extension offices, community promotion 1 hour
Shari’ah-Compliant Financial Products Murabaha, Musharakah, Mudarabah, Wadiah, Takaful 2-4 hours

Shari’ah compliance in financial training. Under BTA Bill 210, cooperatives in the Bangsamoro must adhere to Shari’ah objectives including halal transactions, no riba (usury), no maysir (gambling), no gharar (excessive speculation), and risk sharing principles.27 If your cooperative offers credit services, your officers and credit committee must understand the Shari’ah-compliant alternatives:

Conventional Product Shari’ah Alternative How It Works
Interest-bearing loan Murabaha (cost-plus financing) The cooperative buys the asset and resells it to the member at a disclosed markup
Interest-bearing savings Wadiah (safekeeping) The cooperative holds deposits as custodian; returns are discretionary gifts, not interest
Insurance Takaful (mutual guarantee) Members contribute to a common fund; claims are paid from the pool, surplus is shared
Joint venture (interest-based) Musharakah (partnership) Both parties contribute capital and share profits and losses proportionally
Investment (guaranteed return) Mudarabah (profit sharing) One party provides capital, the other provides labor/expertise; profits are shared by agreement

10.2.4 Level 400 (Advanced): Strategic Growth and Federation Building

Level 400 is for leaders who will shape the cooperative movement. This tier covers strategic leadership, policy development, movement building, and specialized programs for unique BARMM contexts.

Cooperative Leadership Course (CLT). Target: Chairpersons, senior officers, and general managers. Duration: 40+ hours.

Module Content
Leadership Styles Transformational, servant, ethical leadership; Moral Governance integration
Strategic Management Planning, execution, evaluation; 5-year development planning
Change Management Leading cooperative transformation; responding to regulatory change
Stakeholder Relations Members, community, LGUs, CSEA, development partners
Advocacy and Policy Influencing cooperative policy; engaging with BTA Bill 210 implementation
Movement Building Federation formation, networking, solidarity purchasing, collective advocacy
BDP 2023-2028 Leadership Implementing Strategy 21 and 22, community promotion, LGU coordination

Combatant Transitional Training – STOCK Project. Target: Former combatants organizing cooperatives. Duration: 24-40 hours.28

Module Content
Cooperative Fundamentals Basic concepts adapted for transitional context
Business Legalization How to legally conduct business through cooperatives
Compliance Orientation Understanding CDA/CSEA requirements
Financial Literacy Basic bookkeeping, budgeting, fund management
Market Access Connecting to markets and value chains
Support Programs Cooperative Financial and Material Support Project access

This is not a generic training program. It is designed for cooperatives whose members are transitioning from armed struggle to economic participation. The curriculum accounts for the reality that many participants have limited formal education, no prior experience with corporate governance structures, and urgent needs for livelihood support. Your cooperative can access this training through the CSEA’s Combatant Transitional Program.29

Social Enterprise Development – LAKAS SE Program. Target: Social enterprise organizers and cooperative leaders exploring the SE model. Duration: 16-24 hours.30

Module Content
Social Enterprise Fundamentals Definition, characteristics, difference from cooperatives
SE Legal Framework BTA Bill 210 Book III provisions, national SE legislation efforts
Social Mission Development Identifying social, cultural, or environmental challenges
Business Model Design Balancing social mission with financial sustainability
Impact Measurement Measuring and reporting social impact
Enabling Policies Strategy 22 alignment, institutionalization pathway

Agri-Cooperative Strengthening. Target: Farmer organizations, agri-cooperatives, and Agrarian Reform Beneficiary Organizations (ARBOs). Duration: 24-40 hours.31

Module Content
Agricultural Value Chain From production to marketing; agro-entrepreneurship (Strategy 11)
LGU Partnership Working with LGUs and development stakeholders
Private Sector Collaboration Partnering with industry players
Climate-Smart Agriculture Climate adaptation, disaster preparedness (Strategy 12)
Young Farmers Program Professionalizing young farmers (Strategy 5)
Market Access BARMM top commodities: seaweed (48.42% national production), cassava (41.70%), rubber, coffee

BARMM is the country’s top seaweed producer and top cassava producer.32 Agri-cooperatives in these value chains have a competitive advantage that training can sharpen into sustainable profitability.

10.3 Mandatory Training Requirements

Not all training is optional. The law mandates specific training for specific people at specific times. This section consolidates every mandatory requirement so you can build a compliance-ready training plan.

MC 2025-09 Training Hours at a Glance. Use this table as your quick reference. It shows every stakeholder category, the required courses, total hours, and what happens if your cooperative does not comply.

Category Required Courses Total Hours Sanction for Non-Compliance
Members (all categories) Fundamentals of Cooperatives (6h) + Cooperative Laws (6h) + Leadership and Values Formation (2h) + Financial Literacy (2h) 16 hours Member obligation; no direct sanction, but cooperative must show action plan during inspection
Officers – Micro cooperatives Cooperative Management and Governance (8h) + Financial Management (8h) + Line of Business (8h) 24 hours COC non-issuance for the cooperative; officer disqualification from any elective or appointive position46
Officers – Small, Medium, Large cooperatives Expanded Governance (16h) + Financial Management (8h) + Line of Business (4h) + Role/Function-Specific (4h minimum) 32 hours minimum COC non-issuance for the cooperative; officer disqualification from any elective or appointive position47
Officers – Savings and Credit (PHP 5M+ deposits) Additional Financial, Risk, and Credit Management +16 hours Same as above; additional non-compliance finding during examination
Officers – CSF cooperatives RA 10744 Startup/Refresher (16h) + Role-Specific (4h+) + Compliance Orientation (4h) +20 hours minimum Same as above
Staff (all cooperatives) G1-G10 encouraged topics (see Section 10.3.4) No prescribed hours No sanctions – encouraged, not mandatory48

Read this table carefully. The consequences column is not hypothetical. The CSEA can deny your Certificate of Compliance and disqualify your officers. Build your training plan around these numbers.

10.3.1 PRS for All Members (Before Registration)

Who must attend: Every prospective member of a primary cooperative must complete a Pre-Registration Seminar before joining.33

When: Before your cooperative files for registration with CSEA. Existing cooperatives must ensure new members attend a PRS or equivalent orientation before the General Assembly approves their membership.

Duration: 3 hours 45 minutes.

Conducted by: CSEA (in BARMM). CDA Regional Extension Offices (outside BARMM).34

Certificate validity: 3 years.35

Cost: Free.

Contact for scheduling PRS in BARMM:

CSEA Office JICA Building, BGC Gov. Gutierrez Avenue Brgy. RH XII, Cotabato City Maguindanao 9600 Phone: (00) 552-1105

10.3.2 Basic Cooperative Training for Officers and Directors

Who must complete this: Every officer of the cooperative – Board of Directors, Secretary, Treasurer, Election Committee, Audit Committee, Ethics Committee, Mediation and Conciliation Committee, other GA-created committees, and the General Manager or CEO.36

When: Within the first half of their term. If your cooperative’s officers serve two-year terms beginning in April, all required training must be completed by October.

What happens if they do not complete it:

  • The officer is disqualified from holding any elective or appointive position in the cooperative.37
  • The cooperative faces non-issuance of its Certificate of Compliance for the succeeding year if it willfully fails to comply with training requirements despite due notice.38

Summary of required hours by cooperative category:

Category Required Training Total Hours
Micro cooperative Advanced Cooperative Course (Governance + Finance + Line of Business) 24 hours (8 per topic)
Small, Medium, Large cooperative Expanded Governance (16) + Financial Management (8) + Line of Business (4) + Role-Specific (4 minimum) 32 hours minimum
Savings and credit cooperative (PHP 5M+ deposits) Additional Financial, Risk, and Credit Management 16 additional hours
CSF cooperative RA 10744 Startup/Refresher + Role-Specific 20 hours minimum

10.3.3 Specialized Training by Committee Assignment

Each committee in your cooperative has distinct functions that require distinct knowledge. The table below maps committee assignments to training content.

Committee Required Training Content Minimum Duration
Audit Committee Internal control systems, audit procedures, financial analysis, report writing, GA accountability 8-16 hours
Election Committee Election rules and procedures, candidate validation, conduct of elections, results certification, protest resolution 4-8 hours
Mediation and Conciliation Committee Conflict resolution, mediation techniques, hearing procedures, decision writing, confidentiality 8-16 hours
Ethics Committee Code of ethics development, ethics case analysis, investigation procedures, sanction recommendations, Moral Governance integration 8-16 hours
Education and Training Committee Training needs assessment, program planning, ETF management, provider coordination, impact evaluation, BDP 2023-2028 training alignment 8-16 hours
Credit Committee (if applicable) Credit policies, loan evaluation, collection procedures, delinquency management, Shari’ah-compliant lending alternatives 16-24 hours

The Education and Training Committee deserves special attention. This is the committee responsible for making everything in this chapter happen. Its members must know how to assess training needs, plan annual programs, manage ETF budgets, coordinate with accredited providers, and evaluate training impact. If your Education and Training Committee members have not been trained themselves, they cannot train anyone else.

10.3.4 Staff Training: The G1-G10 Track

MC 2025-09, Section VI.3 encourages – but does not require – cooperatives to provide onboarding and continuous training for hired staff.49 No hours are prescribed. No sanctions apply. But cooperatives that neglect staff development pay for it in turnover, errors, and weak member service.

The MC lists ten encouraged topics. Adapt them to your staff’s actual roles.

Code Topic What It Covers
G1 Basic Cooperative Orientation Cooperative principles, values, organizational structure, and the staff role within the cooperative
G2 Sales Product and service promotion, member engagement, market awareness
G3 Fraud Detection and Prevention Recognizing fraud indicators, internal controls, reporting procedures
G4 Records Management Filing systems, document retention, member records, regulatory documentation
G5 Office Management Workplace organization, supplies management, scheduling, administrative procedures
G6 Store Management Inventory control, merchandising, point-of-sale operations (for consumer and multi-purpose cooperatives)
G7 Customer Service Management Member relations, complaint handling, service quality, communication skills
G8 Bookkeeping Basic accounting, transaction recording, CDA Standard Chart of Accounts, financial record-keeping
G9 Basic Computer Literacy Computer operations, office software, basic data management, digital tools for cooperatives
G10 Data Privacy Data Protection Act (RA 10173) compliance, member data handling, privacy policies and procedures

The G1-G10 list is illustrative. MC 2025-09 uses the phrase “may include but not limited to.” Add topics your staff actually needs. A cashier needs G8 (Bookkeeping) and G10 (Data Privacy). A field coordinator needs G2 (Sales) and G7 (Customer Service). Do not force every staff member through all ten.

Authorized providers for staff training: CDA (free for Bookkeeping), accredited CTPros, certified CDOs (free of charge), and federations for their affiliates.50

Key distinction from officer training. Officers face disqualification for skipping required training. Staff face no regulatory sanction. But a cooperative with untrained staff will underperform – and the Board will bear the consequences when inspection findings cite weak internal controls, poor records, or member complaints.

10.4 Designing and Delivering Training in Your Cooperative

Knowing what training is required is only half the job. The other half is making it happen – assessing needs, finding providers, scheduling sessions, and documenting results. This section gives you the practical tools.

10.4.1 Assessing Training Needs

Before you spend a single peso from the ETF, assess what your cooperative actually needs. A training needs assessment identifies the gap between what your members and officers know now and what they need to know to perform their roles effectively.

Training Needs Assessment Checklist. Use this checklist at the start of each fiscal year. The Education and Training Committee should lead the process with input from the Board of Directors and the General Manager.

Part A: Compliance Requirements

  • ☐ Have all current officers completed the required Advanced Cooperative Course (24 or 32 hours)?
  • ☐ Have officers of savings and credit cooperatives (PHP 5M+ deposits) completed the 16-hour specialized training?
  • ☐ Have all committee members completed role/function-specific training (minimum 4 hours)?
  • ☐ Have all members admitted in the past 12 months completed the Fundamentals of Cooperatives Course (16 hours)?
  • ☐ Is the cooperative’s PRS certificate current (valid for 3 years)?
  • ☐ Has the cooperative adopted an annual continuing education program for members and officers?

Part B: Knowledge and Skills Gaps

  • ☐ Can your Treasurer read and interpret the cooperative’s financial statements?
  • ☐ Can your Audit Committee conduct an internal audit without external assistance?
  • ☐ Do your Board members understand the difference between policy-making and management?
  • ☐ Can your General Manager prepare a budget proposal with cash flow projections?
  • ☐ Do your members understand their rights and obligations under the bylaws?
  • ☐ Can your Credit Committee evaluate loan applications using documented criteria?
  • ☐ Do your officers understand Shari’ah compliance requirements for cooperative transactions?
  • ☐ Can your cooperative prepare and submit the CAPR and CAFS without errors?

Part C: Strategic Development Needs

  • ☐ Does your cooperative have a 5-year strategic plan?
  • ☐ Are officers trained in BDP 2023-2028 alignment (Goal 2, Objective 2.5)?
  • ☐ Does your cooperative know how to access PESO Project support?
  • ☐ Are members aware of market opportunities in BARMM’s top commodities?
  • ☐ Has your cooperative explored Shari’ah-compliant financial products?
  • ☐ Do officers know how to coordinate with the Municipal Cooperative Officer (MCO)?

Part D: Training Infrastructure

  • ☐ Does your cooperative have a designated Education and Training Committee?
  • ☐ Has the committee prepared a training calendar for the current fiscal year?
  • ☐ Does the cooperative have a list of accredited training providers in the region?
  • ☐ Is the ETF balance sufficient to cover planned training activities?
  • ☐ Does the cooperative maintain a training records file?

Scoring. Count the unchecked boxes. Each unchecked box represents a training gap. Prioritize as follows:

Unchecked Boxes Priority Level Action
Part A gaps Critical – address within 30 days Schedule mandatory training immediately; non-compliance risks COC denial and officer disqualification
Part B gaps High – address within 90 days These gaps cause operational errors, audit findings, and governance failures
Part C gaps Medium – address within 180 days These gaps limit growth and miss strategic opportunities
Part D gaps Foundational – address within 60 days Without training infrastructure, no other training can happen

10.4.2 Designing Training for BARMM Learners

BARMM has the lowest functional literacy rate in the Philippines at 71.6 percent.51 Tawi-Tawi is at 33.2 percent – meaning two out of three adults cannot functionally read and write.52 Standard CDA training materials assume Metro Manila literacy levels. They will not work here without adaptation.

The five BARMM learner personas. The CSEA Training Package identifies five composite learner profiles that represent the actual range of cooperative members, officers, and staff in the Bangsamoro.53 Use them to anchor every training design decision.

Persona Profile Role Education Max Passive Segment
Hadji Musa 62, male, Maguindanaon farmer, Datu Odin Sinsuat Board of Directors member (Micro coop) Grade 6 completed 12 minutes
Bai Norhata 45, female, Maranao sari-sari store operator, Marawi City Treasurer (Small coop) High school graduate 12 minutes
Hadji Abdulnasser 55, male, Tausug fisherman, Simunul, Tawi-Tawi (GIDA) Chairperson (Small coop) High school level 12 minutes
Bai Sittie Aminah 38, female, Maguindanaon GM, Cotabato City General Manager/CEO (Medium coop) BS Business Administration 20 minutes
Akmad 24, male, Maguindanaon cashier, Cotabato City Store staff (Medium coop) Senior High School (ABM track) 15 minutes

What “maximum passive segment” means. This is the longest stretch a trainer should talk without an activity, question, or group exercise. For low-literacy learners (Hadji Musa, Bai Norhata, Hadji Abdulnasser), never exceed 12 minutes of passive instruction. For advanced learners (Bai Sittie Aminah), you can stretch to 20 minutes. For young digital-literate staff (Akmad), keep it to 15 minutes. Exceed these limits and your audience checks out.

Five design rules for BARMM training.

  1. Use oral and visual instruction as the primary mode for Personas 1-3. Text-heavy slides and handouts fail when a third of the audience has limited reading ability.
  2. Deliver in the learner’s strongest language. Use Maguindanaon, Maranao, Tausug, Sama, or Iranun – not English – as the primary language of instruction for community-level training.
  3. Open every topic with a real scenario the learner recognizes. Fishing expenses for Tausug cooperatives. Market trading for Maranao cooperatives. Rice farming for Maguindanaon cooperatives.
  4. Schedule around prayer times and Ramadan. Adjust session length, break timing, and energy-level expectations during fasting months.
  5. Design for GIDA constraints. Island municipalities have no reliable internet, limited electricity, and boat-dependent schedules. Your module must work offline with printed and visual materials only.

The BARMM Calibration Checklist. Before delivering any training module in the Bangsamoro, run it through the 21-item BARMM Calibration Checklist (see Appendix ☑). The checklist covers five domains: Linguistic Adaptation (5 items), Cultural Responsiveness (5 items), Economic Contextualization (4 items), Infrastructure Considerations (4 items), and Transition Context (3 items). A module scoring below 15 out of 21 has significant gaps and should be revised before delivery.54

10.4.3 Partnering with CSEA, LGU-CDOs, and Accredited Trainers

Your cooperative does not have to deliver all training in-house. In fact, for mandatory training, you cannot deliver it in-house – only authorized providers may conduct required training.39

CSEA (Cooperative and Social Enterprise Authority). In BARMM, CSEA serves as the primary regulatory and development agency for cooperatives. CSEA conducts:

  • Pre-Registration Seminars (free)
  • Technical assistance visits (free)
  • Compliance-related orientations (free)
  • Specialized programs through the PESO Project, STOCK Project, and LAKAS SE Program

Contact CSEA early. Slots for PRS sessions fill quickly, especially during cooperative registration season (January-March). Ask CSEA about their quarterly training calendar.

Cooperative Development Officers (CDOs) at LGUs. Under RA 11535, every municipal, city, and provincial government must have a Cooperative Development Officer.40 In BARMM, most LGUs have not yet appointed MCOs – which is one of the key challenges the BDP 2023-2028 identifies.41 But where CDOs exist, they are authorized to conduct certain required trainings free of charge, provided they hold a Certificate of Authority from the CDA and have completed the required training-of-trainers orientation.

CDOs can assist your cooperative by:

  • Providing venue and logistical support for PRS sessions
  • Conducting the Advanced Cooperative Course (for micro cooperatives)
  • Coordinating with CSEA on your cooperative’s behalf
  • Facilitating access to LGU cooperative development programs

Accredited Cooperative Training Providers (CTPros). Only CDA-accredited CTPros may conduct fee-based required training. Before engaging a CTPro, verify their accreditation. Ask for:

  • CDA accreditation certificate (must be current)
  • At least 2 years of organizational existence
  • At least 5 qualified trainers on staff
  • Adoption of the standard training curricula prescribed in CDA MC 2025-09, Annex A42

Federations and unions. Your cooperative’s federation can conduct required training for its affiliates. This is often the most cost-effective option because the federation pools CETF remittances from multiple cooperatives and delivers training at scale. Check whether your federation offers an annual training calendar. If it does, align your cooperative’s training plan with the federation schedule to reduce costs.

10.4.4 Recording and Reporting Training Activities

Every training activity must be documented. This is not optional paperwork – it is a compliance requirement and your cooperative’s proof that officers and members have met their training obligations.

For every training session, maintain:

Document Purpose Retention
Attendance sheet Proves who attended; names, organization, address, gender Permanent
Certificates of completion Proves successful completion; includes provider name and accreditation Permanent
Training materials Documents what was taught; presentation slides, handouts, manuals 5 years minimum
Pre-test and post-test results Measures learning gain; baseline vs. post-training knowledge 5 years minimum
Evaluation forms Measures participant satisfaction and training effectiveness 5 years minimum
Provider accreditation proof Proves the provider was authorized to conduct the training Permanent
ETF expenditure receipts Proves funds were used for authorized purposes Subject to audit retention rules

Reporting requirements. Under CDA MC 2025-09, all cooperatives must submit a list of trainings undertaken by officers to the Regional Office (or CSEA in BARMM) in accordance with the form prescribed by the Supervision and Examination Division.43 Records of trainings attended by members and staff must be kept by the cooperative and made available during inspection.

Training records management tip. Create a Training Records File with three sections:

  1. Officers – sorted by name, showing all training completed, dates, providers, and certificates
  2. Members – sorted alphabetically, showing FCC completion date and any additional training
  3. ETF Utilization – chronological record of all ETF expenditures with receipts and matching attendance sheets

Keep this file where your Secretary and Education and Training Committee Chair can access it at any time. When the CSEA inspector arrives, this file should be the first thing you hand over.

10.5 Cross-Skill Development

The first four sections of this chapter covered compliance-driven training – the training the law requires. This section covers something different: training that gives your cooperative a competitive edge. Three methodologies from the business and development world can transform how your cooperative identifies problems, designs solutions, and measures impact.

10.5.1 Design Thinking for Cooperative Product Development

Design thinking is a structured method for solving problems by starting with the people who experience the problem. It has five phases: Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, and Test. For cooperatives, this methodology is powerful because it forces you to design products and services around what your members and customers actually need – not what your Board assumes they need.

How to apply design thinking in your cooperative:

Phase 1 – Empathize. Go to your members. Do not send a survey. Visit their farms, their homes, their workplaces. Ask what problems they face. Ask what they wish the cooperative could do. Listen for the problems they do not articulate – the ones you see in their daily struggles.

Example: A seaweed cooperative in Tawi-Tawi conducted home visits and discovered that members’ biggest problem was not the price of seaweed – it was the 3-day delay getting their harvest to the buyer in Zamboanga. The members never mentioned logistics because they assumed it was unsolvable. The cooperative did not assume. It investigated.

Phase 2 – Define. Turn your observations into a clear problem statement. “Our members lose 15-20 percent of their seaweed harvest value because of a 3-day logistics gap between harvest and buyer delivery.”

Phase 3 – Ideate. Generate multiple solutions. Do not commit to the first idea. Options might include: a cooperative-owned boat, a refrigerated storage facility, a partnership with a logistics company, or a direct buyer arrangement that eliminates the middleman.

Phase 4 – Prototype. Test the simplest version of your best idea. Before investing millions in a refrigerated facility, rent a cold storage unit for one harvest cycle. Measure whether the 3-day delay decreases and whether member income increases.

Phase 5 – Test. Evaluate the results. Did member income increase? By how much? Was the cost sustainable? Refine and repeat.

Training your Education and Training Committee in design thinking takes 8-16 hours. The return is a cooperative that solves real problems instead of guessing at solutions.

10.5.2 Theory of Change for Cooperative Impact Measurement

A Theory of Change (ToC) is a roadmap that connects your cooperative’s activities to the outcomes you want to achieve. It answers the question: “If we do X, then Y will happen, because of Z.”

Most cooperatives operate without a Theory of Change. They conduct activities – training, lending, marketing – without a clear logic connecting those activities to measurable results. When the CSEA or a funding partner asks, “What impact has your cooperative achieved?” they cannot answer with data. They answer with stories.

Stories matter. But data wins funding.

A simple Theory of Change for a BARMM agricultural cooperative:

Element Description
Problem Members earn below PHP 15,000/month from cassava farming due to low yields and no value-added processing
Activities (1) Train 50 members in improved cassava cultivation, (2) Establish a cooperative-owned cassava chip processing facility, (3) Secure market contracts with 3 regional distributors
Outputs 50 members trained, 1 processing facility operational, 3 market contracts signed
Outcomes Member cassava yields increase by 30%, value-added products generate 50% higher revenue per kilogram, members reach PHP 20,000/month average income
Impact Cooperative members lifted above the poverty line; BARMM poverty incidence in the community reduced
Assumptions Members will adopt improved techniques; market demand for cassava chips is sufficient; cooperative can secure startup capital for the facility

Align your ToC with BDP 2023-2028 targets. Your Theory of Change becomes more powerful when it connects to the Bangsamoro Development Plan. If your cooperative’s ToC targets poverty reduction, link it to the BDP’s target of reducing poverty incidence from 29.80% to 20-25%.44 If it targets cooperative profitability, link it to Objective 2.5.1 (profitability of cooperatives increased).45 Funding partners and government agencies want to see alignment.

10.5.3 Lean Canvas for Cooperative Business Modeling

The Lean Canvas is a one-page business model that forces you to answer nine questions about your cooperative’s business on a single sheet of paper. It was designed for startups, but it works equally well for cooperatives launching a new product, service, or business line.

The nine Lean Canvas boxes, adapted for cooperatives:

Box Question Cooperative Application
1. Problem What are the top 3 problems your members or customers face? Low income, limited market access, no value-added processing
2. Customer Segments Who are your members and customers? 200 cassava farmers in Maguindanao del Norte, regional food distributors
3. Unique Value Proposition What makes your cooperative’s offering different? “Farm-to-shelf cassava chips – cooperative-grown, community-processed, halal-certified”
4. Solution How does your cooperative solve each problem? Processing facility, direct market contracts, member training
5. Channels How do you reach your customers? Direct delivery to distributors, cooperative store, trade fairs
6. Revenue Streams How does the cooperative earn? Cassava chip sales, member patronage, processing fees
7. Cost Structure What are the major costs? Equipment, raw materials, labor, transport, packaging
8. Key Metrics What numbers tell you the business is working? Monthly production volume, member income change, customer retention
9. Unfair Advantage What can your cooperative do that competitors cannot easily copy? Member loyalty, cooperative land access, BARMM seaweed/cassava production dominance

When to use Lean Canvas in your cooperative:

  • Before launching a new product line
  • Before applying for PESO Project support
  • Before requesting a loan from a cooperative bank
  • When preparing your cooperative’s annual business plan
  • When evaluating whether to expand into a new market

Training your officers in Lean Canvas takes 4-8 hours and pays for itself the first time your cooperative avoids investing in a business idea that sounded good in a Board meeting but collapses under scrutiny on a one-page canvas.

10.6 Recommended and Voluntary Training Programs (H1-H15)

MC 2025-09, Section VII lists fifteen recommended training topics for officers, members, and staff.55 These are voluntary. No hours are prescribed. No sanctions apply. But cooperatives that pursue them build capabilities their competitors lack.

Code Recommended Training Topic
H1 Organizational Development and Management
H2 Project Development and Management
H3 Basic Computer Literacy
H4 Digitalization
H5 Succession Planning
H6 Franchising
H7 Cooperative Business Continuity and Resiliency
H8 Conflict Management
H9 Strategic Planning
H10 Audit Management
H11 Records Management
H12 Entrepreneurial and Business Management
H13 Cooperative Performance Standards
H14 Risk Management and Business Continuity Management
H15 Other trainings (e.g., Intellectual Property Rights)

The H1-H15 list is illustrative. MC 2025-09 uses the phrase “including but not limited to.” Your cooperative can add any topic that serves its development needs.

How to prioritize. Start with your Training Needs Assessment (Section 10.4.1). Match unchecked boxes in Part C (Strategic Development Needs) to H1-H15 topics. A cooperative that lacks a strategic plan needs H9 (Strategic Planning) before it needs H6 (Franchising). A cooperative with weak records needs H11 (Records Management) before H4 (Digitalization).

For BARMM cooperatives, four topics deserve early attention:

  1. H7 (Business Continuity and Resiliency) – natural disasters, conflict disruptions, and pandemic-era lessons make this essential for Bangsamoro cooperatives.
  2. H8 (Conflict Management) – cooperatives in post-conflict communities face internal disputes rooted in clan dynamics, political affiliations, and transitional tensions.
  3. H9 (Strategic Planning) – align your 5-year plan with BDP 2023-2028 targets to unlock CSEA and development partner support.
  4. H14 (Risk Management) – cooperatives handling savings and credit in GIDA areas face unique risks that standard risk frameworks do not address.

Fund these from your ETF. Recommended training is a valid ETF expenditure. Document attendance, provider credentials, and costs the same way you document required training.

Training Impact Metrics: Connecting Training to BDP 2023-2028 Targets

Track your training outcomes against the measurable targets in the Bangsamoro Development Plan. This is how you demonstrate that your ETF spending is producing results – not just certificates.

Metric Baseline BDP Target How Training Contributes
Cooperative compliance rate 11.66% 50% PRS, FCC, and officer training ensure cooperatives meet regulatory requirements
Cooperative profitability Low (sector-wide) Increased Financial management, business line training, and Lean Canvas improve business decisions
Established social enterprises Limited Increased LAKAS SE training builds SE capacity within cooperatives
Combatant cooperative functionality Struggling Functional STOCK Project training addresses specific transitional gaps
LGU cooperative coordination Inadequate (most LGUs have no MCOs) Improved Officer training includes LGU coordination modules
Poverty incidence 29.80% 20-25% Productive cooperatives generate member income above the poverty line

Chapter Summary: What You Must Do Now

This chapter covered a lot of ground. Here is what matters most, in order of urgency.

Immediate (within 30 days):

  1. Check whether all current officers have completed required training. If not, schedule the training immediately. Officers without training are disqualified from office.
  2. Verify your Education and Training Committee is constituted and trained. Without this committee, nothing in this chapter can happen.
  3. Review your ETF balance. Know how much you have and how much you have spent.

Short-term (within 90 days):

  1. Complete the Training Needs Assessment Checklist in Section 10.4.1. Identify every gap.
  2. Contact CSEA and your federation to schedule training for officers and members who need it.
  3. Create a training records file with three sections: Officers, Members, ETF Utilization.

Medium-term (within 180 days):

  1. Develop your annual training plan. Map every required training to a specific date, provider, and budget.
  2. Ensure all members admitted in the past year have completed the 16-hour Fundamentals of Cooperatives Course.
  3. Introduce cross-skill development – start with one Design Thinking workshop or one Lean Canvas session for your Board.

Ongoing:

  1. Adopt a continuing education program for both officers and members, reflected in your annual development plan.
  2. Track training outcomes against BDP 2023-2028 targets. Report impact, not just attendance.
  3. Remit 50% of CETF to your federation or union annually. Demand the three required schedules in return.

Education, training, and information are not the fifth principle by accident. They are the principle that makes the other six possible. Voluntary membership means nothing if members do not understand what they joined. Democratic control is hollow if officers do not know how to govern. Economic participation fails if the Treasurer cannot read a financial statement. Invest in your people. Everything else follows.

A 21-item quality assurance tool across five domains: Linguistic Adaptation (5 items), Cultural Responsiveness (5 items), Economic Contextualization (4 items), Infrastructure Considerations (4 items), and Transition Context (3 items). Scoring interpretation: 19-21 = Fully Calibrated; 15-18 = Mostly Calibrated; 10-14 = Partially Calibrated; Below 10 = Needs Calibration. ↩

Back to Table of Contents
11
Growth, Merger, Division, and Dissolution

Chapter 11 — Growth, Merger, Division, and Dissolution

Every cooperative reaches a crossroads. Growth brings complexity – your original fifteen members are now three hundred, your single barangay operation now serves three municipalities, and the Articles of Cooperation you filed five years ago no longer describe what your cooperative actually does. Two struggling cooperatives in the same barangay might be stronger as one. A cooperative that has grown too large might serve its members better as two. And a cooperative that has lost its purpose needs a dignified exit – not an embarrassing cancellation order from the CSEA.

This chapter covers all of these transitions. You will learn how to expand your cooperative’s reach, how to merge with or absorb another cooperative, how to divide one cooperative into two, how to join a federation, and how to dissolve your cooperative properly when the time comes. Each of these actions requires specific voting thresholds, legal filings, and member protections. Get them wrong, and the transition fails – or worse, exposes your officers to personal liability.

The law does not punish cooperatives for changing. It punishes cooperatives that change without following the process.

11.1 Growing Your Cooperative

Growth is not a single event. It is a series of decisions – each one requiring a different legal action. Adding twenty new members is straightforward. Expanding into a neighboring municipality requires amending your Articles of Cooperation. Launching a new business line means changing your cooperative type. Each of these changes follows a different path.

11.1.1 Expanding Membership and Area of Operation

Your cooperative’s area of operation is defined in your Articles of Cooperation.1 If your Articles say your cooperative operates in Barangay Pagalungan, Municipality of Datu Odin Sinsuat, Maguindanao del Norte – then that is your legal boundary. You cannot open a branch office in Cotabato City or recruit members from Sultan Kudarat without amending those Articles first.

When you need to amend your area of operation:

  • You want to recruit members from a neighboring barangay or municipality
  • You plan to open a satellite office or collection point outside your registered area
  • A local government unit (LGU) requests your cooperative to serve an expanded territory
  • Your members have migrated but still want to participate

The amendment process requires a two-thirds (2/3) vote of all members with voting rights.2 This is not two-thirds of those present at the General Assembly. It is two-thirds of your entire voting membership. If you have 150 voting members, you need at least 100 votes in favor.

Step-by-step: Amending your area of operation

1. DRAFT the proposed amendment
   (specify the new area of operation in full)
         |
2. NOTIFY all members at least 30 days
   before the Special General Assembly
         |
3. PRESENT the amendment at the
   Special General Assembly
         |
4. VOTE — requires 2/3 of ALL members
   with voting rights
         |
5. CERTIFY the amendment
   (secretary and majority of directors,
   under oath)
         |
6. FILE with the CSEA within 30 days
   of approval
         |
7. WAIT for CSEA approval
   (effective upon approval, or after 30
   days if CSEA does not act for reasons
   not attributable to the cooperative)

Practical tip. Before expanding your area, conduct an economic survey of the new territory. How many potential members live there? What services do they need? Can your cooperative’s current capital base support the expansion? Growth without preparation is how cooperatives overextend and fail.

BARMM example. A multipurpose cooperative based in Wao, Lanao del Sur, wants to serve members in the neighboring municipality of Bumbaran. The cooperative calls a Special General Assembly, secures a two-thirds vote from its 200 voting members (at least 134 votes needed), certifies the amendment, and files it with the CSEA Lanao del Sur Extension Office. Thirty days later, the amendment takes effect and the cooperative can legally operate in both municipalities.

11.1.2 Adding New Business Lines (Amending the AOC)

Your cooperative’s type determines what business activities you can legally conduct.3 A credit cooperative lends money. A consumer cooperative procures and distributes goods. A multipurpose cooperative combines two or more types. If your credit cooperative wants to start a consumer store, you must amend your Articles to become a multipurpose cooperative – or register the store as a separate entity.

The same two-thirds (2/3) vote requirement applies.4 The same filing process applies. But adding a business line carries additional requirements:

New Business Line Additional Requirements
Credit/lending Minimum paid-up share capital; compliance with BSP or CSEA lending regulations
Consumer store Business permit from the LGU; inventory management system
Production/processing FDA or BFAD permits (if food); DENR clearance (if manufacturing)
Insurance Insurance Commission accreditation; higher capitalization
Banking BSP license; significantly higher capital requirements

The critical question: amend or register a new cooperative? Use this decision guide:

Is the new business line closely related
to your current operations?
     |
     +-- YES --> Amend your AOC to become
     |           multipurpose (if not already)
     |
     +-- NO ---> Consider these factors:
                  |
                  +-- Will the new line require
                  |   different membership?
                  |   YES --> Register separately
                  |
                  +-- Will the new line expose
                  |   your existing assets to
                  |   new risks?
                  |   YES --> Register separately
                  |
                  +-- Can your current capital
                      base support both lines?
                      NO --> Register separately

Warning. Operating a business line not authorized in your Articles of Cooperation is a compliance violation. The CSEA can issue a deficiency notice, withhold your Certificate of Compliance, and – in serious cases – initiate cancellation proceedings.5

11.2 Merger and Consolidation

Two cooperatives become one. The law provides two paths: merger (one cooperative absorbs the other) and consolidation (both cooperatives dissolve and form an entirely new one).6 The legal effects are identical. The practical difference is which name survives.

11.2.1 When Merger Makes Sense

Merger is not a sign of failure. It is a strategic decision. Consider merger when:

  • Two cooperatives serve the same community with overlapping services and competing for the same members
  • One cooperative has strong management but weak capital, while another has capital but poor governance
  • Membership has declined in both cooperatives to near the minimum threshold (15 members for a primary)
  • Economies of scale would reduce operating costs – one accountant instead of two, one office instead of two, one audit fee instead of two
  • A federation or the CSEA recommends it after identifying duplication in your area

When merger does NOT make sense:

  • The cooperatives serve different types of members with different needs
  • One cooperative carries undisclosed liabilities that would burden the surviving entity
  • The cultures are incompatible – one is run democratically, the other by a single family
  • Members of one cooperative do not want to join the other

BARMM example. Two credit cooperatives operate in the same municipality of Upi, Maguindanao del Norte. Each has 40 members, a part-time bookkeeper, and annual net surplus of less than PHP 50,000. Their combined overhead – rent, audit fees, CSEA filings – consumes most of their earnings. The CSEA extension officer recommends they merge. After both cooperatives vote separately, they consolidate into a single credit cooperative with 80 members, a full-time bookkeeper, and the financial strength to access institutional lending programs.

11.2.2 Legal Requirements and Voting Thresholds

The merger or consolidation process is governed by PB-210, Section 18 (for Bangsamoro cooperatives) and RA 9520, Article 21 (for the national framework).7 The requirements are strict because merger permanently ends the existence of at least one cooperative.

Voting requirement: three-fourths (3/4) of all members with voting rights, present and constituting a quorum, at separate General Assembly meetings of each constituent cooperative.8 Each cooperative votes independently. If either cooperative fails to reach the threshold, the merger cannot proceed.

Requirement Detail
Who votes All members with voting rights in each cooperative, separately
Threshold 3/4 of members present, with quorum
Dissenting members May withdraw their membership and receive a refund of their share capital9
Filing Submit merger plan and resolutions to the CSEA
Effective date Upon issuance of the Certificate of Merger or Consolidation by the CSEA10

Step-by-step: The merger process

1. NEGOTIATE the terms of merger
   (which cooperative survives, how
   assets and liabilities transfer,
   officer positions in the merged entity)
         |
2. DRAFT a Merger Plan
   (include financial statements of both
   cooperatives, proposed AOC of the
   surviving/new cooperative, timeline)
         |
3. NOTIFY all members of BOTH
   cooperatives at least 30 days
   before their respective GAs
         |
4. HOLD SEPARATE General Assemblies
   in each cooperative
         |
5. VOTE in each GA — requires 3/4
   of members present with quorum
         |
6. ALLOW dissenting members to withdraw
   (refund share capital per bylaws)
         |
7. FILE the following with the CSEA:
   - Certified copies of both resolutions
   - Merger Plan
   - Financial statements of both cooperatives
   - Proposed AOC of the surviving/new cooperative
   - List of members of the merged entity
         |
8. CSEA reviews and issues the
   Certificate of Merger or Consolidation
         |
9. MERGER takes effect on the date
   of the Certificate

Practical tip. The Merger Plan is the most important document. It should address every question a member might ask: What happens to my share capital? Will my loan terms change? Who will be on the new board? Where will the office be? A clear Merger Plan reduces dissent and increases the likelihood of reaching the three-fourths vote.

11.2.3 Effects of Merger on Members and Creditors

The law is clear about what happens after merger. Under PB-210, Section 19 and RA 9520, Article 22, five effects occur automatically:11

  1. The constituent cooperatives become one. In a merger, the surviving cooperative continues. In a consolidation, a new cooperative exists.

  2. The separate existence of absorbed cooperatives ceases. Their registration numbers become inactive. Their names are removed from the CSEA roster.

  3. The surviving cooperative inherits everything. All assets, rights, privileges, immunities, and franchises of each constituent cooperative transfer to the surviving or consolidated entity.

  4. The surviving cooperative inherits all liabilities. Every debt, every obligation, every pending lawsuit. The merger does not erase what was owed. Creditors can pursue the surviving cooperative as if it had incurred those obligations itself.12

  5. Creditor rights are protected. No lien on property of any constituent cooperative is impaired by the merger. No pending legal action is dismissed. The surviving cooperative steps into the shoes of every constituent.

What Transfers What Does NOT Transfer
All assets and property Nothing – everything transfers
All liabilities and debts
All pending lawsuits
All contracts and agreements
All member share capital
All regulatory obligations

Warning for members. If you vote for merger with a cooperative that has hidden debts, your cooperative absorbs those debts. Insist on a full financial audit of both cooperatives before voting. This is not optional. It is the single most important protection you have.

11.3 Division of Cooperatives

The opposite of merger. One cooperative becomes two or more. Division is rarer than merger, but the law provides for it because cooperatives sometimes outgrow their original purpose or territory.

11.3.1 When and Why to Divide

Consider division when:

  • The cooperative has grown so large that members in distant areas feel disconnected from governance
  • Two distinct business lines within the cooperative would each be stronger as independent entities
  • Internal conflict between geographic or sectoral groups has made unified governance impossible
  • Regulatory requirements favor smaller, specialized cooperatives over a single large multipurpose entity

BARMM example. A multipurpose cooperative in Cotabato City has 500 members – half are market vendors running a consumer store, half are government employees running a credit operation. The two groups have different needs, different risk profiles, and different meeting schedules. After years of tension over how to allocate net surplus, the General Assembly votes to divide into two cooperatives: a consumer cooperative for the vendors and a credit cooperative for the employees. Each group gets the assets and liabilities related to its business line.

11.3.2 The Three-Fourths Vote Requirement

Division requires a resolution approved by three-fourths (3/4) of all members with voting rights, present and constituting a quorum.13 The procedure is prescribed by CSEA regulations, but the law imposes two firm conditions:

  1. Each new cooperative must comply with all registration requirements. Minimum 15 members, minimum paid-up share capital, Articles of Cooperation, bylaws – everything starts fresh for each new entity.14

  2. No division in fraud of creditors. You cannot divide a cooperative to dump its debts into one entity and move its assets to another. Any division that prejudices creditors is void.15

Step-by-step: The division process

1. PROPOSE the division
   (identify which members, assets, and
   liabilities go to each new cooperative)
         |
2. DRAFT a Division Plan
   - Asset allocation between new entities
   - Liability allocation (fair and
     proportional, not in fraud of creditors)
   - Member assignment (by choice or
     by business line)
   - Timeline for transition
         |
3. NOTIFY all members at least 30 days
   before the General Assembly
         |
4. HOLD a General Assembly
         |
5. VOTE — requires 3/4 of members
   present, with quorum
         |
6. ALLOW dissenting members to withdraw
         |
7. EACH new cooperative completes the
   full registration process with the CSEA
   (AOC, bylaws, bond, economic survey)
         |
8. CSEA reviews and registers each
   new cooperative
         |
9. The original cooperative ceases
   to exist upon registration of the
   new entities

Asset and liability allocation. This is the hardest part of any division. The Division Plan must allocate assets and liabilities proportionally and fairly. A common approach:

Allocation Method How It Works Best For
By business line Each new cooperative gets the assets and liabilities of its business line Multipurpose cooperatives splitting by type
By membership proportion Assets and liabilities split based on member count or share capital Geographic splits
By negotiation Members negotiate a custom allocation through the GA Complex situations with mixed assets

Warning. Creditors must be notified and their claims honored. If Cooperative A owes PHP 500,000 to a bank and divides into Cooperatives B and C, the bank must know which entity assumes the debt – and must consent to the arrangement. Division without creditor protection is legally void.16

11.4 Forming or Joining a Federation

Your cooperative does not have to stand alone. Federations and unions are secondary and tertiary cooperative structures that allow cooperatives to pool resources, share expertise, and speak with a collective voice.17

11.4.1 Requirements for Federation Membership

Under PB-210, Section 21, a federation requires a minimum of three registered cooperatives of the same type of service within the Bangsamoro region.18 Three credit cooperatives can form a credit federation. Three multipurpose cooperatives can form a multipurpose federation. You cannot mix types – a credit cooperative, a consumer cooperative, and a producer cooperative cannot form a single federation.

To form a federation, carry out the same formalities required for registering a primary cooperative:

  • Articles of Cooperation for the federation
  • Bylaws
  • Economic survey demonstrating the need for the federation
  • Bond for accountable officers
  • List of member cooperatives with their registration numbers

To join an existing federation, your cooperative must:

  1. Pass a board resolution or General Assembly resolution (depending on your bylaws) authorizing membership
  2. Meet the federation’s admission requirements (financial standing, compliance status, cooperative type)
  3. Pay the federation’s membership fee and commit to regular dues
  4. Submit your most recent CAPR, CAFS, and Certificate of Compliance as proof of good standing

11.4.2 Benefits and Obligations of Federation Membership

A federation is not just a registry. It is a working organization with specific functions defined by law.19

What your federation does for you:

Federation Function What It Means for Your Cooperative
Complementary business activities The federation can run wholesale operations, central warehousing, or bulk purchasing that your cooperative cannot do alone
Education and advisory work Training programs, management consulting, technical assistance – often free or subsidized for members
Bookkeeping and accounting support Standardized systems, shared software, audit preparation assistance
Coordination Joint marketing, shared logistics, inter-cooperative lending
Joint ventures Access to national and international partnerships your cooperative could not pursue alone
Publications Newsletters, market information, regulatory updates

What you owe your federation:

  • 50% of your Education and Training Fund (ETF) goes to your chosen federation or union every year20
  • Annual dues as set by the federation’s General Assembly
  • Compliance with federation bylaws and resolutions
  • Timely submission of reports the federation needs for its own compliance

Cooperative Unions serve a different purpose. While federations are organized by type of business, unions are organized by territory – provincial, city, regional, or national.21 Unions represent cooperatives of all types within their jurisdiction. They advocate for cooperative interests with the government, conduct research, and promote cooperative principles.

Your cooperative can belong to both a federation (by type) and a union (by territory) simultaneously.

BARMM example. The Bangsamoro Cooperative Union represents cooperatives of all types across the region. A credit cooperative in Marawi City might belong to both the Lanao del Sur Credit Cooperative Federation (by type) and the Bangsamoro Cooperative Union (by territory). The federation helps it with lending practices and shared capital. The union represents its interests in regional policy discussions.

11.5 Voluntary Dissolution

Sometimes a cooperative must end. Members move away. The business becomes unviable. Internal conflict makes governance impossible. The law provides a structured process for voluntary dissolution – one that protects members and creditors while allowing the cooperative to close its affairs with dignity.

There are two paths, and the distinction is critical: dissolution where no creditors are affected and dissolution where creditors are affected.22

11.5.1 The Dissolution Process Under Law

Path 1: No Creditors Affected

If your cooperative has no outstanding debts – no loans, no unpaid suppliers, no pending obligations – the dissolution process is simpler.23

Step Action Who
1 Board resolution approving dissolution Majority of the Board of Directors
2 Notice to members by registered mail or personal delivery, at least 30 days before the GA Secretary
3 Publication of the notice for 3 consecutive weeks in a local newspaper (or newspaper of general circulation if no local paper exists) Board
4 General Assembly vote – requires 3/4 of all members with voting rights, present and constituting a quorum Members
5 Certification of the resolution by a majority of directors, countersigned by the board secretary Directors, Secretary
6 Filing with the CSEA Chairperson
7 CSEA issues Certificate of Dissolution CSEA

Path 2: Creditors Affected

If your cooperative has outstanding debts, the process adds layers of creditor protection.24

1. BOARD RESOLUTION approving dissolution
   (majority of directors)
         |
2. GENERAL ASSEMBLY VOTE
   (3/4 of members present, with quorum)
         |
3. FILE a PETITION for dissolution
   with the CSEA, signed by majority
   of directors, verified by chairperson
   or secretary
   - Must list ALL claims and demands
     against the cooperative
         |
4. CSEA issues an ORDER
   - Sets a hearing date (30-60 days out)
   - Order is published weekly for 3
     consecutive weeks in a local newspaper
   - Order is posted in 3 public places
     in the cooperative's municipality
         |
5. CREDITORS have 5 days to file
   OBJECTIONS after the notice period
         |
6. CSEA HEARING
   - Tries any issues raised in objections
         |
7. If petition is proven, CSEA issues
   ORDER OF DISSOLUTION setting forth:
   - Assets and liabilities
   - Claims of each creditor
   - Number of members
   - Nature and extent of member interests

The critical difference. In Path 1, the CSEA issues the certificate after receiving your resolution. In Path 2, the CSEA conducts a hearing, allows creditors to object, and only dissolves the cooperative after satisfying itself that creditor rights are protected.

11.5.2 Settling Liabilities and Distributing Remaining Assets

Dissolution is not the end. It is the beginning of liquidation – a three-year wind-down period during which the cooperative continues to exist for the sole purpose of settling its affairs.25

During the three-year liquidation period, the cooperative may:

  • Prosecute and defend lawsuits
  • Settle and close its affairs
  • Dispose of, convey, and distribute its properties and assets
  • Convey all properties to trustees for the benefit of members, creditors, and other persons in interest

The order of distribution matters. Assets are distributed in this priority:

1. TAXES and government obligations
         |
2. SECURED CREDITORS
   (those with liens on specific assets)
         |
3. UNSECURED CREDITORS
   (suppliers, lenders without collateral)
         |
4. MEMBER SHARE CAPITAL
   (refund of paid-up shares)
         |
5. RESERVE FUND
   (does NOT go to members -- must be
   placed in a usufructuary trust for
   the benefit of the cooperative's
   federation or union)[^26]
         |
6. REMAINING NET SURPLUS
   (distributed to members based on
   patronage or as the GA resolves)

Three rules you must not break:

  1. No asset distribution before all debts are paid. A cooperative shall only distribute its assets upon lawful dissolution and after payment of all its debts and liabilities.27

  2. The Reserve Fund is never distributed to members. Upon dissolution, the General Assembly may resolve to establish a usufructuary trust fund for the benefit of the federation or union, or donate it to a cooperative institution.28

  3. Unclaimed assets go to the federation or union. If a creditor or member is unknown or cannot be found, their share goes to the federation or union with which the cooperative is affiliated.29

BARMM example. A fishermen cooperative in Tawi-Tawi with 30 members decides to dissolve. It has PHP 200,000 in a bank loan, PHP 50,000 in unpaid supplier invoices, and PHP 800,000 in total assets (including a motorized banca and a cold storage unit). The cooperative files a petition with the CSEA because creditors are affected. After the hearing, the CSEA issues a dissolution order. During the three-year liquidation, the cooperative sells the banca and cold storage, pays the bank (PHP 200,000), pays the supplier (PHP 50,000), refunds member share capital (PHP 300,000), transfers the Reserve Fund (PHP 80,000) to the Tawi-Tawi Fishermen Cooperative Federation, and distributes the remaining PHP 170,000 to members based on patronage.

11.6 Involuntary Dissolution and Cancellation of Registration

Not every dissolution is voluntary. The CSEA and the courts have the power to end a cooperative’s existence when it violates the law or fails to operate. Understanding these grounds protects you from them.

11.6.1 Grounds for Cancellation

The law provides three paths to involuntary dissolution.30

Path 1: Court-Ordered Dissolution

A competent court may dissolve a cooperative after due hearing on two grounds:

  1. Violation of any law, regulation, or provision of its bylaws
  2. Insolvency31

Path 2: CSEA-Ordered Cancellation of Registration

The CSEA may suspend or revoke a cooperative’s Certificate of Registration, after due notice and hearing, on any of the following grounds:32

Ground What It Means
Fraudulent registration The cooperative obtained its COR through false documents, fake member lists, or misrepresentation
Illegal purpose The cooperative exists for a purpose that violates the law – money laundering, illegal lending, front for other activities
Willful Code or bylaw violations The cooperative has been notified of violations and continues to violate despite warning
Willful failure to operate on a cooperative basis The cooperative is run as a private business, not according to cooperative principles (one-member-one-vote, democratic governance, etc.)
Failure to meet minimum membership The cooperative has fallen below 15 members (for a primary) and has not recruited new members within a reasonable period

Path 3: Dissolution by Failure to Organize and Operate

This is the most common involuntary dissolution in BARMM. Under PB-210, Section 61(a), a cooperative that has not commenced business within one year of registration, or has not carried on its business for one year, triggers intervention.33

The timeline under PB-210 is more structured than the national law:

REGISTRATION DATE
      |
      v
6 MONTHS: If cooperative has not
started operations, CSEA intervenes
with support, guidance, and monitoring
      |
      v
12 MONTHS: If still not operating,
CSEA denies the Certificate of
Compliance (COC) -- cooperative
becomes officially INACTIVE
      |
      v
2 YEARS without operations:
CSEA sends a SHOW CAUSE notice.
Cooperative must explain why it
has not operated.
      |
      v
If no justifiable cause is provided:
CSEA DELETES the cooperative from
the roster. It is deemed DISSOLVED.

Under the national framework (RA 9520, Article 68), the threshold is two years without commencing business or two consecutive years without operations.34 PB-210 is stricter – it begins intervention at six months and flags inactivity at one year.

Due process is required. The CSEA cannot simply delete your cooperative from the roster. It must send a formal notice. It must give you the opportunity to show cause. Only after you fail to provide a justifiable reason will the CSEA proceed with dissolution.35

11.6.2 The Inactive Cooperative Problem and Reactivation

BARMM has thousands of registered cooperatives that exist only on paper. They registered, received a COR, and never operated – or operated for a year and went silent. These inactive cooperatives distort the data, occupy registration numbers, and give a false picture of the cooperative movement’s strength.

How cooperatives become inactive:

Cause Frequency in BARMM
Organizers lost interest after registration Very common
Key officers migrated or moved away Common
Capital was never collected from members Common
No viable business in the community Occasional
Internal conflict paralyzed operations Occasional
Disaster or displacement disrupted the community Occasional (but significant in conflict-affected areas)

Can an inactive cooperative be reactivated? Yes – but only before the CSEA has formally dissolved it. If your cooperative is inactive but still on the CSEA roster, take these steps:

  1. Reconvene the Board of Directors. If a quorum is not possible, call a Special General Assembly to elect new directors.

  2. Conduct an inventory of all assets, liabilities, and member records.

  3. File all overdue reports – CAPR, CAFS, and any other required submissions. Pay any outstanding penalties.

  4. Adopt a reactivation plan with a clear business program, updated economic survey, and realistic timeline.

  5. Request CSEA assistance. Under PB-210, Section 61(a), the CSEA is required to provide support, assistance, guidance, and close monitoring to cooperatives that have not started operations within six months.36 Use this mandate. The CSEA extension officer in your area can help you develop a business plan, connect you with training providers, and monitor your progress.

  6. Apply for a new Certificate of Compliance once you have filed all reports and resumed operations.

The reactivation decision. Before investing effort in reactivation, ask honestly:

Do you still have at least 15 members
willing to participate actively?
     |
     +-- NO --> Dissolve voluntarily. It is
     |          better to end cleanly than
     |          to accumulate penalties.
     |
     +-- YES
          |
     Is there a viable business
     opportunity for the cooperative?
          |
          +-- NO --> Dissolve voluntarily.
          |          A cooperative without a
          |          business is a club, not
          |          a cooperative.
          |
          +-- YES
               |
          Can you settle all outstanding
          debts and penalties?
               |
               +-- NO --> Negotiate with
               |          creditors first.
               |          Reactivation with
               |          unpaid debts exposes
               |          new officers to old
               |          liabilities.
               |
               +-- YES --> REACTIVATE.
                           File reports,
                           pay penalties,
                           resume operations.

BARMM example. A consumer cooperative in Basilan registered in 2022 but never operated because its founding members were displaced by flooding. By 2024, the members have returned and want to start the cooperative. The cooperative is still on the CSEA roster but has not filed any reports. The members reconvene, elect a new board, file their overdue CAPR (reporting zero operations), pay the late filing penalties, and submit a reactivation plan with an updated economic survey to the CSEA Extension Office in Basilan. The CSEA assigns a Cooperative Development Specialist to monitor their progress for six months.

Summary: Choosing Your Path

Every transition described in this chapter begins with the same question: What is best for the members? Not what is easiest for the officers. Not what avoids hard conversations. What genuinely serves the people who entrusted their share capital to this cooperative.

Situation Best Path Key Requirement
You need to serve a larger area or add services Amend your AOC (Section 11.1) 2/3 vote of all members
Two cooperatives would be stronger as one Merge (Section 11.2) 3/4 vote in each cooperative, separately
One cooperative should become two Divide (Section 11.3) 3/4 vote; no fraud on creditors
You want collective bargaining power and shared services Join a federation (Section 11.4) Board or GA resolution; good compliance standing
The cooperative has no future Dissolve voluntarily (Section 11.5) 3/4 vote; settle all debts first
The cooperative has been inactive for over a year Reactivate or dissolve (Section 11.6) Honest assessment of viability

One final principle. The law does not trap you in a cooperative that no longer works. It gives you tools to grow, to combine, to split, and to close. But every tool requires member consent, creditor protection, and proper filing. Skip any of those three, and your transition is not just invalid – it is a violation.

Use the tools. Follow the process. Protect the members.

Footnotes

  1. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 11(a)(3) – the Articles of Cooperation must state the area of operation of the cooperative. ↩

  2. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 15 – amendment of Articles of Cooperation requires a two-thirds (2/3) vote of all members with voting rights. See also RA 9520, Article 18. ↩

  3. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 20 – defines 21 types of cooperatives in the Bangsamoro region, from credit to workers cooperatives. ↩

  4. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 15 – the same two-thirds voting requirement applies to any amendment, whether changing the area of operation, business type, or other provisions. ↩

  5. PB-210, Book I, Chapter V, Section 61(e) – the CSEA may revoke registration for willful violation of the Code or bylaws despite notice. ↩

  6. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 18 – two or more cooperatives may merge into a single cooperative which shall either be one of the constituent cooperatives (merger) or the consolidated cooperative (consolidation). See also RA 9520, Article 21. ↩

  7. RA 9520, Article 21 – Merger and Consolidation of Cooperatives. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 18 – Merger and Consolidation of Cooperatives. Both laws contain substantially similar provisions, with PB-210 adapted for the Bangsamoro context under the CSEA. ↩

  8. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 18(b) – three-fourths (3/4) vote of all members with voting rights, present and constituting a quorum, at separate general assembly meetings of each constituent cooperative. ↩

  9. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 18(b) – dissenting members may withdraw their membership pursuant to Section 27, Chapter II. See also RA 9520, Article 21(2), which references Article 30 on withdrawal rights. ↩

  10. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 18(c) – the merger or consolidation shall be effective upon the issuance of the certificate of merger or consolidation by the CSEA. ↩

  11. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 19 – Effects of Merger and Consolidation. See also RA 9520, Article 22. ↩

  12. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 19(e) – the surviving cooperative shall be responsible for all liabilities and obligations of each constituent cooperative. Neither the rights of creditors nor any lien upon property shall be impaired by the merger. ↩

  13. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 17 – division requires a three-fourths (3/4) vote of all members with voting rights, present and constituting a quorum. See also RA 9520, Article 20. ↩

  14. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 17 – all requirements set forth in the Code must be complied with by the new cooperatives resulting from division. ↩

  15. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 17 – “no division of a cooperative in fraud of creditors shall be valid.” See also RA 9520, Article 20. ↩

  16. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 17. The prohibition against fraudulent division means creditors must be informed and their claims properly allocated among the resulting cooperatives. ↩

  17. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 21 (Federations) and Section 22 (Unions). See also RA 9520, Articles 24 and 25. ↩

  18. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 21 – “Three or more registered cooperatives in the Region of the same type of services may form a federation.” ↩

  19. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 21, paragraphs 1-7 – enumerates seven functions of a federation, from business activities to coordination to joint ventures. See also RA 9520, Article 24. ↩

  20. PB-210, Book I, Chapter VI, Section 76(b) – the Education and Training Fund allocation, of which 50% goes to the cooperative’s chosen federation or union. See also RA 9520, Article 86. ↩

  21. PB-210, Book I, Chapter I, Section 22 – cooperative unions represent the interest and welfare of all types of cooperatives at the provincial, city, regional, and national levels. See also RA 9520, Article 25. ↩
