  22. PB-210, Book I, Chapter V, Section 61(b) and 61(c) – the two paths for voluntary dissolution. See also RA 9520, Articles 64 and 65. ↩

  23. PB-210, Book I, Chapter V, Section 61(b) – voluntary dissolution where no creditors are affected. See also RA 9520, Article 64. ↩

  24. PB-210, Book I, Chapter V, Section 61(c) – voluntary dissolution where creditors are affected. See also RA 9520, Article 65. ↩

  25. PB-210, Book I, Chapter V, Section 61(g) – liquidation of a cooperative. The cooperative continues to exist for three years after dissolution for the purpose of winding up its affairs. See also RA 9520, Article 69. ↩

  26. PB-210, Book I, Chapter VI, Section 76(a) – upon dissolution, the reserve fund shall not be distributed among members. The General Assembly may resolve to establish a usufructuary trust fund for the benefit of the federation or union. ↩

  27. PB-210, Book I, Chapter V, Section 61(g)(3) – a cooperative shall only distribute its assets or properties upon lawful dissolution and after payment of all its debts and liabilities. See also RA 9520, Article 69. ↩

  28. PB-210, Book I, Chapter VI, Section 76(a) – the reserve fund upon dissolution may be placed in a usufructuary trust fund for the benefit of any federation or union to which the cooperative is affiliated. ↩

  29. PB-210, Book I, Chapter V, Section 61(g)(2) – upon the winding up of cooperative affairs, any asset distributable to any creditor or shareholder who is unknown or cannot be found shall be given to the federation or union to which the cooperative is affiliated. See also RA 9520, Article 69. ↩

  30. PB-210, Book I, Chapter V, Section 61(d), (e), and (f) – three involuntary dissolution paths: court-ordered, CSEA-ordered, and failure to organize and operate. ↩

  31. PB-210, Book I, Chapter V, Section 61(d) – involuntary dissolution by court order on grounds of law/bylaw violation or insolvency. See also RA 9520, Article 66. ↩

  32. PB-210, Book I, Chapter V, Section 61(e) – five grounds for CSEA cancellation of registration. See also RA 9520, Article 67. ↩

  33. PB-210, Book I, Chapter V, Section 61(a) – dissolution by failure to operate, with the CSEA intervention timeline beginning at six months. ↩

  34. RA 9520, Article 68 – dissolution by failure to organize and operate within two years of registration or two consecutive years of non-operation. ↩

  35. PB-210, Book I, Chapter V, Section 61(a) and (f) – the CSEA must send a formal notice and allow the cooperative to show cause before deleting it from the roster. ↩

  36. PB-210, Book I, Chapter V, Section 61(a) – “the Authority will intervene to provide support, assistance, guidance and close monitoring to help the cooperative to operate within the next six (6) months.” ↩

Back to Table of Contents
12
Dispute Resolution, Penalties, and Legal Protection

Chapter 12 – Dispute Resolution, Penalties, and Legal Protection

Disputes are inevitable. Your cooperative brings together people with shared interests but different temperaments, expectations, and levels of commitment. A farmer who joined for credit access sees the world differently from a teacher who joined for savings. A board member who wants aggressive expansion will clash with a treasurer who insists on conservative reserves. The question is not whether disputes will arise – it is whether your cooperative has the mechanisms to resolve them before they destroy trust.

Most cooperatives that fail in BARMM do not fail because of bad business plans. They fail because an internal conflict went unresolved, factions formed, members stopped attending assemblies, and the cooperative bled out quietly. A loan dispute between two members escalates into a board fight. A board fight becomes a faction war. A faction war empties the general assembly. An empty general assembly means no quorum, no elections, no audit approval, and eventually no cooperative.

This chapter gives you the tools to break that cycle. You will learn the formal dispute resolution mechanisms that the law requires, the liability rules that hold your officers accountable, the criminal penalties that punish abuse, and the documentation practices that protect your cooperative when things go wrong. Every cooperative needs these mechanisms in place before the first dispute – not after.

12.1 Types of Cooperative Disputes

Not all disputes are the same. The law distinguishes between disputes that arise within a cooperative and disputes that arise between cooperatives. The distinction matters because each type follows a different resolution path.

12.1.1 Intra-Cooperative Disputes (Member vs. Member, Member vs. Board)

Intra-cooperative disputes are the most common. They occur among members, officers, directors, and committee members of the same cooperative.1 These disputes typically fall into five categories:

Financial disputes. A member claims the cooperative miscalculated her patronage refund. A borrower disputes the interest charged on his loan. A departing member demands immediate refund of share capital that the bylaws say must be held for six months. These disputes are about money, and they generate the most heat.

Election disputes. A candidate claims the Election Committee disqualified him unfairly. A losing candidate alleges irregularities in the vote count. A group of members demands a special election because the annual election lacked a quorum. Election disputes are time-sensitive – they must be resolved before the new board takes office or the cooperative operates in legal limbo.

Governance disputes. A member accuses a director of conflict of interest. The Audit Committee reports unauthorized expenditures by the General Manager. A group of members files a petition to remove the Chairperson for alleged mismanagement. Governance disputes test the integrity of your cooperative’s leadership.

Disciplinary disputes. A member faces expulsion for violating the bylaws. A borrower is sanctioned for defaulting on a loan. A director is suspended for missing three consecutive board meetings without excuse. Disciplinary actions always carry due process requirements – skip them, and the action is void.

Policy disputes. Members disagree on whether the cooperative should expand into a new line of business. The board and the membership clash over a proposed bylaw amendment. A faction wants to merge with a neighboring cooperative; another faction opposes it. Policy disputes are about direction, and they belong in the general assembly – not in back-channel meetings.

Dispute Type Common Trigger Resolution Body
Financial Loan terms, refunds, patronage allocation Mediation Committee, then Board
Election Disqualification, vote count, quorum Election Committee, then GA
Governance Conflict of interest, unauthorized spending Ethics Committee, then Board, then GA
Disciplinary Bylaw violation, default, misconduct Board (investigation), then GA (removal)
Policy Expansion, merger, bylaw amendment General Assembly

12.1.2 Inter-Cooperative and Inter-Federation Disputes

Inter-cooperative disputes arise between two or more cooperatives – or between a cooperative and its federation or union.2 These are less common but more complex. A primary cooperative disputes the service fees charged by its federation. Two cooperatives in the same municipality claim overlapping service areas. A federation suspends a member-cooperative for non-payment of dues, and the suspended cooperative challenges the suspension.

The same conciliation-mediation-arbitration process applies to inter-cooperative disputes. The difference is that the cooperative union or federation acts as the first-level mediator before the dispute escalates to the CSEA.3

12.2 The Conciliation and Mediation Process

The law is clear: disputes must first be settled amicably.4 You cannot skip straight to the courts. You cannot file a complaint with the CSEA without first going through your cooperative’s own conciliation and mediation mechanisms. This is not optional. It is a jurisdictional prerequisite.

The dispute resolution process follows a mandatory sequence. Understand it. Follow it. Skipping a step invalidates everything that follows.

Dispute Resolution Process

STEP 1: BYLAWS-BASED MECHANISMS
Aggrieved party files written complaint
with the Mediation and Conciliation Committee
                    |
                    v
STEP 2: COMMITTEE MEDIATION (30 days)
Committee facilitates dialogue,
proposes settlement terms
                    |
          +---------+---------+
          |                   |
      SETTLED             NOT SETTLED
   (Agreement signed)        |
                              v
STEP 3: FEDERATION/UNION CONCILIATION
If cooperative belongs to a federation,
dispute goes to federation mediation
                    |
          +---------+---------+
          |                   |
      SETTLED             NOT SETTLED
   (Agreement signed)        |
                              v
STEP 4: CERTIFICATION OF FAILURE
Committee + federation issue certification
that all amicable efforts have failed
                    |
                    v
STEP 5: VOLUNTARY ARBITRATION (CSEA)
File complaint with CSEA for
voluntary arbitration
(Attach failure certification)
                    |
                    v
STEP 6: APPEAL
Arbitration decision appealable
to the Office of the President

12.2.1 Bylaws-Based Dispute Resolution Mechanisms

Your bylaws must contain a conciliation or mediation mechanism.5 This is not a suggestion. BTA Bill 210 requires it as a mandatory bylaw provision. If your bylaws do not include a dispute resolution clause, add one at your next general assembly.

A strong bylaws-based dispute resolution clause covers seven elements:

  1. Who can file. Any regular or associate member, any officer, any committee member. Specify that the complaint must be in writing and state the facts clearly.

  2. Where to file. All complaints go to the Mediation and Conciliation Committee. Do not allow complaints filed directly with the board – that bypasses the process.

  3. Timeline. Set a deadline for the committee to act. Thirty calendar days from receipt of the complaint is standard. If the committee does not act within that period, the complainant may escalate.

  4. Hearing procedure. Both parties must be heard. The committee schedules a joint session, allows each side to present its position, and facilitates dialogue toward a settlement.

  5. Settlement agreement. If the parties reach agreement, it must be in writing, signed by both parties and the committee, and filed with the board. A signed settlement agreement is binding.

  6. Failure to settle. If mediation fails, the committee issues a certification of non-settlement. This certification is a prerequisite for filing with the CSEA.

  7. Confidentiality. All mediation proceedings are confidential. Statements made during mediation cannot be used as evidence in subsequent proceedings.

Sample bylaws provision:

Article [__]. Dispute Resolution. – All disputes among members, officers, directors, and committee members shall be submitted to the Mediation and Conciliation Committee for amicable settlement within thirty (30) calendar days from filing. If settlement fails, the Committee shall issue a Certification of Non-Settlement, after which the aggrieved party may seek voluntary arbitration through the CSEA. No member may file a complaint with the CSEA or the courts without first exhausting the internal dispute resolution process.

12.2.2 The Mediation and Conciliation Committee

The Mediation and Conciliation Committee is one of the mandatory committees your cooperative must maintain.6 The board of directors appoints its members. It must have at least three members, and none of them should be parties to the dispute they are mediating.

Composition requirements:

  • Minimum three members
  • Appointed by the Board of Directors
  • Cannot include the Chairperson or General Manager (they are too often parties to disputes)
  • Members should have training in mediation or conflict resolution
  • Serve for a term set by the bylaws (typically matching the board’s term)

Functions of the Committee:

Function What It Means in Practice
Receive complaints Accept written complaints from any member, officer, or committee member. Log the complaint in the Dispute Register.
Investigate Gather facts from both parties. Review relevant documents – loan records, meeting minutes, financial reports.
Mediate Bring both parties together. Facilitate dialogue. Propose settlement terms.
Document Record every step – the complaint, the investigation, the mediation sessions, the outcome.
Certify If mediation fails, issue a Certification of Non-Settlement signed by all committee members.
Report Submit a semi-annual report to the board on all disputes filed, resolved, and pending.7

Practical tip. Your Mediation Committee is only as effective as its members’ skills. Send your committee members to mediation training. The CSEA and accredited training providers offer conflict resolution programs. A trained mediator can resolve in two sessions what an untrained one cannot resolve in ten.

12.2.3 When to Escalate to CSEA or the Courts

Escalation is not failure. Some disputes cannot be resolved internally because the stakes are too high, the parties are too entrenched, or the issue involves legal questions that a mediation committee cannot answer.

Escalate to the CSEA when:

  • Your Mediation Committee has issued a Certification of Non-Settlement
  • Your federation or union (if applicable) has also certified that its mediation efforts failed
  • The dispute involves interpretation of the Cooperative Code or your registration documents
  • The dispute involves allegations of financial mismanagement that require forensic examination

Escalate to the courts when:

  • The dispute involves criminal acts (fraud, embezzlement, falsification of records)
  • The dispute involves claims against third parties who are not cooperative members
  • The CSEA’s voluntary arbitration has been exhausted and a party appeals to the Office of the President

Do not escalate prematurely. The CSEA will reject any complaint that is not accompanied by a Certification of Non-Settlement from your Mediation Committee and (if applicable) your federation.8 Filing without these certifications wastes time and money.

The jurisdiction of the CSEA’s voluntary arbitrators is exclusive and original over cooperative disputes that have exhausted internal mediation.9 This means the regular courts cannot hear these disputes until the CSEA process is complete. Decisions of voluntary arbitrators are appealable to the Office of the President – not to the Court of Appeals or the Regional Trial Court.

12.3 Liability of Directors, Officers, and Committee Members

Your cooperative’s officers are not just volunteers doing community service. The law treats them as fiduciaries – people entrusted with other people’s money and interests. When they betray that trust, they answer personally.

This section explains when personal liability attaches, what disloyalty looks like, and how your cooperative removes an officer who has violated the trust placed in them.

12.3.1 When Personal Liability Attaches

Directors, officers, and committee members are personally liable when they commit any of three acts:10

1. Voting for or assenting to patently unlawful acts. If the board votes to disburse the reserve fund for personal bonuses – a clear violation of the law – every director who voted “yes” is personally liable. Every director who was present and did not object is deemed to have assented. The word “patently” means the unlawfulness is obvious. You do not need a lawyer to tell you that stealing from the reserve fund is wrong.

2. Gross negligence or bad faith in directing cooperative affairs. A treasurer who never reconciles the bank account is grossly negligent. A chairperson who signs blank checks for the General Manager to fill in later is acting in bad faith. Gross negligence is not an honest mistake – it is a failure so extreme that no reasonable person in that position would have made it.

3. Acquiring personal or pecuniary interest in conflict with their duty. A director who awards a supply contract to his own business without disclosure has a conflict of interest. A committee member who uses her access to member records to solicit clients for her insurance business has a conflict of interest. The test is simple: did the officer benefit personally from a decision they made in their official capacity?

When liability attaches, it is joint and several.11 This means the cooperative (or a damaged member) can collect the full amount of damages from any one liable officer – not just a proportional share. If three directors approved an unlawful disbursement of PHP 300,000, the cooperative can collect the entire PHP 300,000 from any one of them.

The double-profits rule. When a director, officer, or committee member acquires any interest or equity adverse to the cooperative in a matter entrusted to them in confidence, they are liable as a trustee for the cooperative. They must return all damages and account for double the profits that would have accrued to the cooperative.12 This is a punitive provision. The law does not just want your money back. It wants to make betrayal painful.

Liability Trigger Example Consequence
Voting for unlawful acts Board approves disbursement from reserve fund for personal bonuses Joint and several liability for all damages
Gross negligence Treasurer fails to reconcile bank accounts for 12 months; PHP 200,000 goes missing Personal liability for losses caused
Bad faith Chairperson signs blank checks for General Manager Personal liability for all unauthorized disbursements
Conflict of interest Director awards supply contract to own business without disclosure Liable for damages + double profits
Breach of confidence Officer uses insider knowledge of cooperative’s expansion plans to buy adjacent land Liable as trustee + double profits

12.3.2 Disloyalty, Confidential Information, and Conflict of Interest

The law separates three specific forms of misconduct that carry enhanced penalties.

Disloyalty. A director who acquires for himself an opportunity that should belong to the cooperative is disloyal.13 The classic case: the cooperative is negotiating to lease a warehouse, and a director quietly leases it for his own business instead. The disloyal director must account for double the profits and refund them to the cooperative. This liability applies even if the director used his own funds for the venture.

The only defense against a disloyalty charge is ratification by three-fourths (3/4) vote of all members with voting rights, present and constituting a quorum.14 The ratification must happen at a general assembly meeting, with full disclosure of what the director did.

Illegal use of confidential information. A director or officer – or their associate – who uses confidential cooperative information for personal benefit is liable for three things:15

  1. Compensating the cooperative for direct losses suffered
  2. Accounting for any direct benefit received or yet to be received
  3. Criminal penalty of a fine of not less than PHP 500,000 or imprisonment of 5-10 years, or both16

“Confidential information” means any information that, if generally known, might reasonably be expected to adversely affect the cooperative’s operation and viability. Member financial records. Pending business deals. Internal audit findings. Loan delinquency lists.

Conflict of interest in contracts. A contract between the cooperative and one of its own directors, officers, or committee members is voidable at the cooperative’s option – unless four conditions are all met:17

  1. The director’s presence was not necessary to constitute a quorum
  2. The director’s vote was not necessary to approve the contract
  3. The contract is fair and reasonable
  4. For officers or committee members, the contract was previously authorized by the GA or the board

If any of the first two conditions is absent, the contract can still be ratified by a three-fourths (3/4) vote of all voting members present and constituting a quorum, provided there is full disclosure of the director’s adverse interest and the contract is fair and reasonable.18

Practical guidance. Require all directors and officers to submit an annual disclosure of interests listing all businesses, properties, and financial interests that could conflict with their cooperative duties. Keep these disclosures on file. When a board decision involves a potential conflict, require the conflicted director to recuse – leave the room, do not participate in the discussion, and do not vote. Record the recusal in the minutes.

12.3.3 Removal Procedures and Due Process

Removal is the ultimate accountability mechanism. It says: you violated the trust placed in you, and you can no longer serve. But removal without due process is itself a violation. Follow the procedure exactly.

Step 1: File the complaint with the Board. All complaints for removal of any elected officer must be filed with the Board of Directors.19 The complaint must be in writing and state specific grounds – not vague accusations.

Step 2: Investigation and hearing. The officer must be given the opportunity to be heard. This is a constitutional right. The board conducts a preliminary investigation. During this investigation, a majority of the board may place the officer under preventive suspension.20 Preventive suspension is not a penalty – it is a precautionary measure to prevent the officer from interfering with the investigation.

Step 3: Prima facie finding. If the board finds prima facie evidence of guilt – meaning enough evidence that a reasonable person would believe the officer committed the act – it presents its recommendation for removal to the General Assembly.21

Step 4: General Assembly vote. The GA decides. An elective officer may be removed by three-fourths (3/4) vote of the regular members present and constituting a quorum, at a regular or special GA called for the purpose.22 The officer must be given another opportunity to be heard at the assembly. This is a separate hearing from the board investigation.

Step 5: Record the decision. Document everything. The resolution of removal, the vote count, the officer’s defense, the evidence presented – all of it goes into the official minutes.

Step Action Who Decides Voting Threshold
1 File written complaint Complainant files with Board N/A
2 Investigation + hearing Board of Directors Majority for preventive suspension
3 Prima facie finding Board of Directors Majority recommendation
4 Removal vote General Assembly 3/4 of members present (with quorum)
5 Record and implement Secretary records; Board implements N/A

Warning. If you remove an officer without following this process, the removal is void. The officer can challenge it, and the cooperative may face liability for damages. Do not shortcut due process even when the evidence of wrongdoing is overwhelming.

12.4 Penal Provisions

The previous section dealt with civil liability – the officer owes the cooperative money. This section deals with criminal liability – the officer goes to jail, pays a fine, or both. The penal provisions of BTA Bill 210 are modeled on RA 9520 but carry updated penalties calibrated for BARMM.

These are not theoretical penalties. They are enforceable criminal offenses. The CSEA can initiate complaints on its own motion. Any cooperative or member can file a criminal case.

12.4.1 Prohibited Acts

BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Chapter II lists seven categories of prohibited acts.23 Study them. Make sure your officers know them. Ignorance of the law excuses no one.

Prohibited Act 1: Unauthorized use of “Cooperative.” No person, group, or organization may use the word “Cooperative” in its name unless it is duly registered as a cooperative under the Code.24 This protects the public from fake cooperatives that collect money under false pretenses.

BARMM example: A group in Cotabato City collects “membership fees” and “share capital” from market vendors under the name “Bangsamoro Vendors Cooperative” without CSEA registration. This is a criminal offense.

Prohibited Act 2: Tax evasion. Any person who willfully evades or defeats the tax provisions of the Code commits a criminal offense.25 This includes cooperatives that claim tax exemptions they are not entitled to, and officers who falsify financial records to maintain an exemption.

Prohibited Act 3: Government interference with tax exemptions. Any public official who deprives a registered cooperative of its lawful tax exemption – by requiring unauthorized fees, denying exemption certificates, or adding conditions not in the law – commits a criminal offense.26

Prohibited Act 4: Government interference with cooperative autonomy. Any public official who meddles in the internal affairs of a cooperative commits an offense.27 This includes:

  • Influencing elections through public or private endorsement
  • Requiring prior clearance for cooperative decisions
  • Demanding creation of positions or recommending appointments
  • Any act that infringes cooperative autonomy

BARMM example: A municipal mayor tells a cooperative’s board that government livelihood funds will only be released if the mayor’s recommended candidate is appointed General Manager. This is a prohibited act, regardless of the mayor’s intentions.

Prohibited Act 5: Officer violations of fiduciary duty. Directors, officers, or committee members who violate the liability provisions (Section 44), the disloyalty provision (Section 45), or the confidential information provision (Section 46) face criminal penalties on top of civil liability.28

Prohibited Act 6: Reporting and record-keeping offenses. Eight specific acts fall under this category:29

  1. Refusing to furnish required information or reports
  2. Submitting false or misleading reports to the CSEA
  3. Refusing to keep required books or registers
  4. Making false entries in required books
  5. Hindering authorized inspections, audits, or examinations
  6. Failing to comply with CSEA orders or written instructions
  7. Violating restrictions on related-party transactions
  8. Abetting or authorizing another person to commit any offense under the Code

Prohibited Act 7: Social enterprise misrepresentation. Any entity that falsely represents itself as a social enterprise to access benefits and incentives commits an offense.30 The officers of such entity face personal liability.

12.4.2 Penalties and Fines Under BTA Bill 210

The penalties vary by offense. The table below consolidates every penalty provision in Book IV, Chapter II of BTA Bill 210.

Prohibited Act Imprisonment Fine Legal Basis
Unauthorized use of “Cooperative” 2-5 years Up to PHP 20,000 BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 731
Tax evasion 2-4 years PHP 30,000-100,000 BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 832
Government obstruction of tax exemption 1-5 years PHP 5,000 minimum BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8(a)33
Government interference with autonomy 2-5 years PHP 20,000 minimum BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 834
Officer fiduciary violations (Secs. 44-46) 5-10 years PHP 500,000 BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8(f)35
Reporting/record-keeping offenses 1-5 years Up to PHP 50,000 BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8(g)36
Catch-all (violations with no specific penalty) 6 months-1 year PHP 1,000 minimum BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8(p)37
SE misrepresentation (individuals) 6 months-2 years PHP 250,000 BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 938
SE misrepresentation (juridical entities) Officers: 2+ years Officers: PHP 500,000; Entity: double tax savings BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 939

Three critical points about these penalties:

First, when a cooperative is the violator, the penalty falls on its directors and officers personally – not on the cooperative as an institution.40 The law pierces the cooperative veil.

Second, public officials who commit violations suffer the accessory penalty of temporary absolute disqualification from holding any public office.41 A barangay chairperson who interferes with a cooperative’s elections does not just face a fine – they lose their position.

Third, the CSEA can initiate complaints motu proprio – on its own initiative, without waiting for a member to file.42 If the CSEA discovers violations during an audit or examination, it can refer the matter directly to prosecutors.

12.5 Protecting Your Cooperative

The best defense against disputes, liability claims, and criminal charges is not a good lawyer. It is good documentation. A cooperative that keeps meticulous records can defend every decision it makes. A cooperative that keeps poor records is vulnerable to every accusation, justified or not.

12.5.1 Documentation and Record-Keeping as Legal Defense

Every document your cooperative produces is potential evidence. Treat it that way. The following records serve as your cooperative’s legal armor:

Board minutes. Record every board meeting. Include the date, attendees, agenda, discussions, motions, votes (including who voted for, against, and abstained), and resolutions. When a director recuses due to conflict of interest, record it. When the board rejects a proposal, record the reasons. Minutes are your primary defense against claims of unauthorized action.

General assembly minutes. Same standard as board minutes, with the added requirement of recording the quorum count and the vote tallies for every resolution. If the GA votes to remove an officer, record the exact count – not just “motion carried.”

Financial records. The Treasurer must maintain complete and current books of account.43 Monthly bank reconciliations. Disbursement vouchers with supporting documents. Collection receipts in sequence. A cooperative that cannot account for every peso it received and spent is a cooperative whose officers face personal liability.

Dispute records. The Mediation and Conciliation Committee must maintain a Dispute Register – a chronological log of every complaint received, the parties involved, the dates of mediation sessions, the outcome, and any Certification of Non-Settlement issued. This register proves that your cooperative followed the mandatory dispute resolution process.

Disclosure records. Keep every annual disclosure of interests submitted by directors and officers. Keep copies of every conflict-of-interest recusal. These records prove that your cooperative took governance seriously.

Communication records. Keep copies of all notices sent to members – meeting notices, election notices, assessment notices, demand letters. Use methods that create a paper trail: registered mail, acknowledged receipt, or electronic transmission with read confirmation. A member who claims “I was never notified” cannot sustain that claim when you produce a signed acknowledgment.

How long to keep records. The Code does not specify a blanket retention period, but apply this rule: keep all records for at least ten years from the date of creation. Financial records should be kept for the cooperative’s entire existence. Dispute records should be kept for at least five years after resolution. When in doubt, do not destroy – store.

12.5.2 Working with CSEA and Legal Counsel

Your cooperative should not wait for a crisis to build its relationship with the CSEA and legal counsel.

CSEA relationship. The Cooperative and Social Enterprise Authority is not just a regulator. It is also a resource. Use it proactively:

  • Register early. File your compliance reports on time. A cooperative with a clean compliance record gets the benefit of the doubt when disputes arise.
  • Seek advisory opinions. If your board is unsure whether a proposed transaction creates a conflict of interest, write to the CSEA for an advisory opinion before proceeding. A CSEA advisory opinion is not binding law, but it demonstrates good faith.
  • Report problems early. If your cooperative discovers internal fraud, report it to the CSEA before the CSEA discovers it during an audit. Self-reporting demonstrates institutional integrity.
  • Use CSEA training programs. The CSEA offers training on governance, financial management, and dispute resolution. Send your officers and committee members.

Legal counsel. Not every cooperative can afford a lawyer on retainer. But every cooperative should know when to engage one:

Situation Do You Need a Lawyer?
Routine mediation of member disputes No – your Mediation Committee handles this
Drafting or amending bylaws Recommended – ensures legal compliance
Removal proceedings for an officer Recommended – due process must be followed precisely
Criminal complaint filed against an officer Yes – immediately
CSEA issues a Show Cause Order Yes – respond within 15 days with legal guidance
Contract review for major transactions Recommended – especially related-party transactions
Dissolution proceedings Yes – protects member interests during liquidation

Cooperative federations and legal pooling. If your cooperative belongs to a federation, explore legal pooling arrangements where member-cooperatives share the cost of a retained lawyer. Five cooperatives each paying PHP 2,000 per month gives the group a PHP 10,000 monthly retainer – enough to secure a competent provincial attorney who can advise all five cooperatives on routine matters and represent them in emergencies.

The Ombudsman option. If a government official or employee violates the cooperative’s rights – by interfering with elections, obstructing tax exemptions, or meddling in internal affairs – your cooperative can file a complaint with the Ombudsman, the Civil Service Commission, or the appropriate court.44 Do not suffer government interference silently. The law protects your cooperative’s autonomy, but only if you invoke that protection.

Chapter Summary

Disputes will come. Officers will make mistakes. Some will make deliberate ones. The question is whether your cooperative has built the mechanisms to handle these situations before they become existential threats.

Five things to do after reading this chapter:

  1. Review your bylaws. Does your dispute resolution clause cover all seven elements listed in Section 12.2.1? If not, draft an amendment for the next GA.

  2. Appoint a trained Mediation Committee. If your current committee has no mediation training, send them to a CSEA-accredited conflict resolution program within 60 days.

  3. Create a Dispute Register. Start logging every complaint from today forward – even informal ones that get resolved quickly. The register is your proof of process.

  4. Require annual disclosure of interests. Every director and officer should submit a written disclosure listing all businesses, properties, and financial relationships that could create conflicts.

  5. Brief your officers on the penal provisions. Every director, officer, and committee member should know that fiduciary violations carry imprisonment of 5-10 years and a fine of PHP 500,000. Ignorance is not a defense.

Your cooperative exists because its members chose to trust each other with their money, their labor, and their aspirations. Protecting that trust is not just good governance. It is the law.

Footnotes

  1. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 4. “Disputes among members, officers, directors, and committee members, and intra-cooperative, inter-cooperative, intra-federation or inter-federation disputes shall, as far as practicable, be settled amicably in accordance with the conciliation or mediation mechanisms embodied in the bylaws.” See also RA 9520, Art. 137. ↩

  2. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 4. The provision covers “intra-cooperative, inter-cooperative, intra-federation or inter-federation disputes.” ↩

  3. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 4, par. 3. “Should such conciliation or mediation proceeding fail, the matter shall be settled through voluntary arbitration: Provided, however, That before any party can validly file a complaint with the Authority for voluntary arbitration, it must first secure a certification from its conciliation and mediation committee and from the cooperative union or federation to which it belongs.” ↩

  4. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 4, par. 1. “Disputes… shall, as far as practicable, be settled amicably.” ↩

  5. BTA Bill 210, Book I, Sec. 15(n). The bylaws must include “a conciliation or mediation mechanism for the amicable settlement of disputes among members, directors, officers and committee members of the cooperative.” See also RA 9520, Art. 15(n). ↩

  6. Governance Guide, Mediation and Conciliation Committee. “Appointed by Board of Directors. Composition: 3 members (minimum). Functions: Hear complaints and grievances against members, officers, or staff; mediate disputes between parties; recommend resolutions.” ↩

  7. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 4. The committee’s reporting obligation is implicit in its duty to facilitate amicable settlement and issue certifications. Best practice: submit semi-annual reports to the board. ↩

  8. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 4, par. 3. No party “can validly file a complaint with the Authority for voluntary arbitration” without first securing certification of failure from the mediation committee and the cooperative union or federation. ↩

  9. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 4, par. 4. “The jurisdiction of the voluntary arbitrators shall be exclusive and original and their decisions shall be appealable to the Office of the President.” ↩

  10. BTA Bill 210, Book II, Sec. 44. “Directors, officers and committee members, who willfully and knowingly vote for or assent to patently unlawful acts or who are guilty of gross negligence or bad faith in directing the affairs of the cooperative or acquire any personal or pecuniary interest in conflict with their duty… shall be liable jointly and severally for all damages or profits resulting therefrom.” See also RA 9520, Art. 45. ↩

  11. BTA Bill 210, Book II, Sec. 44. Liability is “jointly and severally.” ↩

  12. BTA Bill 210, Book II, Sec. 44, par. 2. “He shall, as a trustee for the cooperative, be liable for damages and shall be accountable for double the profits which otherwise would have accrued to the cooperative.” See also RA 9520, Art. 45, par. 2. ↩

  13. BTA Bill 210, Book II, Sec. 45. “A director who, by virtue of his office, acquires for himself an opportunity which should belong to the cooperative shall be liable for damages and must account for double the profits.” See also RA 9520, Art. 48. ↩

  14. BTA Bill 210, Book II, Sec. 45. Ratification requires “a three-fourths (3/4) vote of all the members with voting rights, present and constituting a quorum.” ↩

  15. BTA Bill 210, Book II, Sec. 46. See also RA 9520, Art. 49. ↩

  16. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8(f). “A director, officer or committee member who violated the Section 44, Chapter III on the liability of Directors, Officers and Committee members, Section 45, Chapter III on the disloyalty of a Director, and Section 46, Chapter III of this Code on the illegal use of confidential information shall upon conviction suffer a fine of not less than Five hundred thousand pesos (P500,000.00)… or imprisonment of not less than five (5) years but not more than ten (10) years or both.” ↩

  17. BTA Bill 210, Book II, Sec. 48. See also RA 9520, Art. 47. ↩

  18. BTA Bill 210, Book II, Sec. 48, par. 2. Ratification requires “three-fourths (3/4) vote of all the members with voting rights, present and constituting a quorum” with “full disclosure of the adverse interest of the directors involved.” ↩

  19. BTA Bill 210, Book II, Sec. 47. “All complaints for the removal of any elected officer shall be filed with the board of directors.” See also RA 9520, Art. 50. ↩

  20. BTA Bill 210, Book II, Sec. 47. “Majority of the board of directors may place the officer concerned under preventive suspension pending the resolution of the investigation.” ↩

  21. BTA Bill 210, Book II, Sec. 47. “Upon finding of prima facie evidence of guilt, the board shall present its recommendation for removal to the general assembly.” ↩

  22. BTA Bill 210, Book II, Sec. 47. “An elective officer may be removed by three fourths (3/4) votes of the regular members present and constituting a quorum, in a regular or special general assembly meeting called for the purpose.” ↩

  23. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Ch. II. “The following acts or omissions affecting cooperatives are hereby prohibited.” ↩

  24. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 7. ↩

  25. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8. ↩

  26. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8(a). ↩

  27. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8, items (a)-(e) on government interference. ↩

  28. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8(f). ↩

  29. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8(g)-(o). ↩

  30. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 9. ↩

  31. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 7. “The penalty of imprisonment of not less than two (2) years nor more than five (5) years and a fine not exceeding twenty thousand (20,000.00) pesos or both.” ↩

  32. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8. “A fine of not less thirty thousand pesos (30,000.00) but not more than one hundred thousand pesos (100,000.00) and suffer imprisonment of not less than two (2) years but not more than four (4) years.” ↩

  33. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8(a). “A penalty not less than one (1) year but not more than five (5) years imprisonment or a fine in the amount of not less than five thousand pesos (P5,000.00) or both.” ↩

  34. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8, final paragraph. “Not less than two (2) years but not more than five (5) years imprisonment or a fine in the amount of not less than Twenty thousand pesos (P20,000.00), or both.” ↩

  35. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8(f). “A fine of not less than Five hundred thousand pesos (P500,000.00)… or imprisonment of not less than five (5) years but not more than ten (10) years or both.” ↩

  36. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8(g). “Imprisonment of not less than one (1) year nor more than five (5) years or a fine of not more than Fifty thousand pesos (P50,000.00) or both.” ↩

  37. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8(p). “Imprisonment of not less than six (6) months nor more than one (1) year and a fine of not less than One thousand pesos (P1,000.00), or both.” ↩

  38. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 9. “Imprisonment for not less than six (6) months but not more than two (2) years or a fine of not less than two hundred fifty-thousand (P250,000.00) or both.” ↩

  39. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 9, par. 3. “Officers of the juridical entity… shall be liable for a fine of P500,000.00 or imprisonment for not less than two (2) years or both.” The entity must “pay double the benefits it reaped.” ↩

  40. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8(o). “In case the violator is a cooperative or juridical person, the penalty shall be imposed on its directors and officers.” ↩

  41. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8, final paragraph. “In the case of a public official or employee, the offender shall upon conviction, suffer the accessory penalty of temporary absolute disqualification.” ↩

  42. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 7, par. 2. “The Authority may motu proprio, initiate complaints for violation of this provision.” ↩

  43. BTA Bill 210, Book III, Ch. IV (Responsibilities of Cooperatives). The cooperative must “keep a complete set of books of account.” See also RA 9520, Art. 52. ↩

  44. BTA Bill 210, Book IV, Sec. 8, par. following Sec. 8(p). “The cooperative or any of its members can file a case against any officer or employee of the Bureau of Internal Revenue or of any other government agency with the Ombudsman, Civil Service Commission, other appropriate government agency or the courts of law.” ↩

Back to Table of Contents
13
The CSEA-BARMM and the Cooperative Ecosystem

Chapter 13 – The CSEA-BARMM and the Cooperative Ecosystem

Your cooperative does not operate in isolation. Behind it stands an entire ecosystem of government agencies, development partners, and fellow cooperatives. Knowing who does what – and how to access their services – is as practical as knowing your bylaws. A cooperative that understands the ecosystem gets faster registration, better training, cheaper financing, and stronger legal protection. A cooperative that does not understand it wastes months navigating bureaucracy, misses grant deadlines, and solves problems alone that the system was designed to solve for them.

This chapter maps the ecosystem. You will learn what the CSEA-BARMM does and how it is structured. You will learn what services you can request – and how to request them. You will learn how your LGU Cooperative Development Officer fits into the picture. You will learn which BTA committees oversee the cooperative sector, how the CDA relates to the CSEA, and which development partners operate in the region. You will also learn about the Combatant Transitional Program – one of the most important cooperative development pathways in the Bangsamoro.

Treat this chapter as your directory. Return to it every time you need to know who to call, what to file, or where to go.

13.1 The Cooperatives and Social Enterprise Authority (CSEA-BARMM)

The Cooperatives and Social Enterprise Authority (CSEA) is the primary government agency responsible for cooperatives and social enterprises in the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region. Every cooperative you register, every compliance certificate you secure, every training program you request – all of these go through or involve the CSEA. Understanding this agency is not optional. It is the foundation of your relationship with the Bangsamoro government’s cooperative development infrastructure.

13.1.1 Mandate, Powers, and Functions (BTA Bill 210, Sections 11-13)

What the CSEA is. Under BTA Bill 210, the CSEA is the sole agency of the Bangsamoro Government authorized to register cooperatives and social enterprises.1 It enjoys fiscal autonomy and is attached to the Office of the Chief Minister.2 This placement is significant. It means the CSEA reports to the highest executive authority in the Bangsamoro government, giving cooperative development a seat at the top of the policy table.

The CSEA’s mandate. The CSEA, together with all branches and agencies of the Bangsamoro Government, must ensure the provision of technical guidance, financial assistance, and other services to enable cooperatives to develop into viable and responsive economic enterprises.3 The operative word is “ensure.” The mandate is not merely to offer services. It is to guarantee that cooperatives have what they need to become platforms for poverty reduction and sustainable, inclusive, and equitable development.

Powers and functions. BTA Bill 210, Section 13 grants the CSEA twenty-four enumerated powers. You do not need to memorize all of them. But you do need to know the categories and which ones directly affect your cooperative:

Planning and policy. The CSEA formulates, adopts, and implements integrated plans and programs on cooperative and social enterprise development.4 These plans must be consistent with the overall socio-economic development plans of the Bangsamoro Government – meaning the Bangsamoro Development Plan (BDP) 2023-2028 and its successors.

Training and capability building. The CSEA develops and conducts management and training programs upon request of cooperatives and social enterprises.5 This is demand-driven. Your cooperative must request the training. Do not wait for the CSEA to come to you. Submit a formal request letter to the CSEA Provincial Office or the Cooperative Development Division at the central office.

Registration and compliance. The CSEA is the sole registration authority. It processes applications for Certificates of Registration (COR) and Certificates of Compliance (COC).6 It also requires all cooperatives to submit annual reports and audited financial statements.7

Financial access. The CSEA assists cooperatives in accessing financial and other forms of assistance under terms designed to strengthen their viability and autonomy.8 It also administers all grants and donations coursed through the Bangsamoro Government for cooperative development.9

Dispute resolution. Upon request of either or both parties, the CSEA mediates and conciliates disputes within a cooperative or between cooperatives.10 This is the escalation path when your internal mediation and conciliation mechanisms fail, as discussed in Chapter 12.

Combatant reintegration. The CSEA promotes and strengthens cooperatives as pathways to reintegrate former combatants, surrenderees, and disaster-affected communities.11 This is a unique Bangsamoro mandate that does not exist at the national level. Section 13.5 of this chapter covers the Combatant Transitional Program in detail.

Coordination. The CSEA coordinates the efforts of LGUs and the private sector in the promotion, organization, and development of cooperatives and social enterprises.12 Your municipal or city government should be working with the CSEA on cooperative programs – not independently.

What this means for you. The CSEA is not just a registration office. It is your cooperative’s primary government partner for training, financing, compliance support, dispute resolution, and market development. Know your CSEA Provincial Director by name. Keep their contact number in your records. Visit the provincial office at least once per quarter – even when you do not have a specific problem. Building a relationship during calm times ensures faster response during urgent ones.

13.1.2 Organizational Structure: Four Divisions and Provincial Offices

The CSEA’s organizational structure is defined by BTA Bill 210, Section 14.13 Understanding it tells you exactly who handles what – so you never send a compliance question to the training division or a legal question to the finance office.

The Executive Director. The CSEA is headed by an Executive Director with the rank of Director III.14 The Executive Director oversees all four divisions, sets agency-wide priorities, and represents the CSEA in Cabinet-level discussions. The Executive Director is assisted by four Division Chiefs and supported by Provincial Directors.

The four divisions. The CSEA is composed of the following divisions:

Division Head Primary Responsibility
Administrative and Finance Division Division Chief Day-to-day operations, budget, administrative support
Cooperative Development Division Division Chief Registration, compliance, training, monitoring, mediation
Social Enterprise Division Division Chief SE promotion, accreditation, product development, education
Shari’ah and Legal Division Division Chief Legal review, Shari’ah compliance, policy research, audits

The Administrative and Finance Division handles the internal machinery of the CSEA – budget management, human resources, procurement, and administrative support.15 You will interact with this division when paying registration fees, requesting official documents, or following up on the administrative status of your applications.

The Cooperative Development Division is the division you will deal with most frequently. It processes registration applications and issues COR and COC certificates.16 It provides mediation and arbitration services.17 It conducts monitoring and inspection of cooperatives.18 It develops and delivers management training programs.19 It assists cooperatives with financing and marketing opportunities.20 And it works on convergence across different ministries and agencies to deliver a whole-of-government approach to cooperative development.21

The Social Enterprise Division handles social enterprise promotion, accreditation, product development, and capacity building.22 If your cooperative also qualifies or intends to qualify as a social enterprise, this is the division that processes your SE accreditation and connects you to the RISE for Peace and Sustainable Development Program. The Social Enterprise Division also encourages social procurement – the practice of government agencies purchasing goods and services from social enterprises.23

Provincial Offices. The CSEA operates Provincial Offices in the provinces of Maguindanao, Lanao del Sur, Basilan, and Tawi-Tawi.24 Each Provincial Office is headed by a Provincial Director with the rank of Director I. Cotabato City and the Special Geographic Area (SGA) fall under the jurisdiction of the CSEA Provincial Office in Maguindanao.25

Your Provincial Office is your primary point of contact for all routine transactions – registration filing, compliance submissions, training requests, and field consultations. For complex matters involving legal interpretation, Shari’ah compliance, or policy disputes, the Provincial Office will coordinate with the relevant division at the central office.

The organizational chart. Think of the structure this way:

Executive Director (Director III)
    |
    |--- Administrative and Finance Division (Division Chief)
    |
    |--- Cooperative Development Division (Division Chief)
    |
    |--- Social Enterprise Division (Division Chief)
    |
    |--- Shari'ah and Legal Division (Division Chief)
    |
    |--- Provincial Office - Maguindanao (Provincial Director, Director I)
    |        (includes Cotabato City and SGA)
    |
    |--- Provincial Office - Lanao del Sur (Provincial Director, Director I)
    |
    |--- Provincial Office - Basilan (Provincial Director, Director I)
    |
    |--- Provincial Office - Tawi-Tawi (Provincial Director, Director I)

How to reach the CSEA central office.

Detail Information
Address Japan-ARMM Friendship Hall and Training Center, Bangsamoro Government Center, Cotabato City64
Email cda@bangsamoro.gov.ph
Phone 552-1105
Website https://csea.bangsamoro.gov.ph
Office Hours Monday-Friday, 8:00 AM - 5:00 PM

Practical tip. When filing documents or making inquiries, always identify which division handles your matter before visiting. A quick phone call to the central office or provincial office saves you from being redirected multiple times on the day of your visit. Bring a reference number for any pending application.

13.1.3 The Shari’ah and Legal Division

The Shari’ah and Legal Division deserves its own section because it represents something unique to the BARMM cooperative ecosystem. No other region in the Philippines has an agency division dedicated to ensuring cooperatives comply with Shari’ah governance. This division is the institutional guarantee that Bangsamoro cooperatives can operate in full alignment with Islamic law.

The division is headed by a Division Chief and performs five core functions:26

Legal and regulatory review. The division assesses whether cooperatives and social enterprises comply with BTA Bill 210, relevant national laws, and regulatory measures issued by the CSEA and other regulatory bodies.27 If your cooperative receives a compliance notice, the Shari’ah and Legal Division is the source of the legal analysis behind it.

Legal and policy research. The division produces legal opinions, bill reviews, policy analyses, notes, and commentaries that inform policy and regulatory measures.28 It also drafts the policies and regulatory directives that the CSEA issues. When a new CSEA circular arrives at your cooperative, this division wrote it – or at least reviewed it for legal soundness.

Shari’ah risk management and control. The division systematically identifies, measures, monitors, and reports Shari’ah non-compliance risks within cooperative operations, business activities, and governance.29 This is not abstract. If your cooperative’s lending practices involve interest (riba), if your investment portfolio includes haram products, or if your contracts contain excessive uncertainty (gharar) – the Shari’ah and Legal Division is the body that flags the problem and recommends corrective action.

Shari’ah research. The division conducts in-depth research and studies on Shari’ah to ensure the CSEA’s regulations are responsive to Islamic jurisprudence.30 This function keeps the regulatory framework current with evolving interpretations and applications of Shari’ah in the cooperative context.

Shari’ah review and audit. The division conducts regular assessments to verify that cooperatives adhere to the Shari’ah governance and compliance framework.31 It also evaluates the CSEA’s own internal control mechanisms, risk management systems, and governance processes for Shari’ah compliance.

What this means for your cooperative. If you operate a Shari’ah-compliant cooperative – and BTA Bill 210 strongly encourages this – the Shari’ah and Legal Division is both your regulator and your resource. Use it. Request Shari’ah compliance reviews before your annual audit, not after. Ask for advisory opinions when you are considering a new financial product or business arrangement. A proactive relationship with this division prevents the kind of compliance surprises that lead to penalties or reputational damage.

13.2 CSEA Services Available to Your Cooperative

The CSEA offers three broad categories of services: regulatory services (registration and compliance), development services (training and technical assistance), and financial services (grants, programs, and market access). Each category includes specific services you can request. This section maps them so you know exactly what is available.

13.2.1 Registration and Compliance Support

Registration services. The CSEA handles the entire cooperative registration process, from name reservation to the issuance of the Certificate of Registration (COR). The specific services include:

  • Cooperative name reservation. You reserve your proposed cooperative name with the CSEA to ensure it is unique and not already taken. The CSEA maintains a masterlist of all registered cooperatives.
  • Processing of registration applications. You submit your Articles of Cooperation, Bylaws, Economic Survey, Treasurer’s Affidavit, Surety Bond, and PRS Certificate to the Provincial Office. The Cooperative Development Division reviews and processes the application.
  • Issuance of the Certificate of Registration. Upon approval, the CSEA issues the COR under its official seal. This certificate is conclusive evidence that your cooperative is duly registered.32
  • Certificate of Amendments. When your cooperative amends its Articles of Cooperation or Bylaws, the CSEA processes and certifies the amendments.

Compliance services. After registration, the CSEA monitors your cooperative’s compliance and issues the critical Certificate of Compliance (COC). The COC is issued after one year of operation and upon compliance with all CSEA requirements.33 Without a valid COC, your cooperative cannot access government programs, enter into contracts with government agencies, or participate in public procurement. The specific compliance services include:

  • Annual report review. The CSEA reviews your Cooperative Annual Performance Report (CAPR) and Cooperative Annual Financial Statement (CAFS).
  • Inspection and validation. CSEA field staff conduct on-site inspections to verify that your cooperative’s actual operations match its reported activities.
  • Compliance assessment. The CSEA evaluates whether your cooperative meets all statutory requirements for COC issuance or renewal.
  • Inactive cooperative intervention. If your cooperative fails to operate within six months of registration, the CSEA intervenes to provide support, assistance, guidance, and close monitoring for the next six months.34 Failure to operate for one full year means the CSEA will deny COC issuance, rendering the cooperative officially inactive.

Practical tip. Do not wait for the CSEA to contact you about compliance. Submit your CAPR and CAFS within 120 days after your fiscal year ends. Hold your General Assembly within 90 days. Track these deadlines on your compliance calendar (see Chapter 9). A cooperative that submits on time gets processed faster and avoids the backlog that hits every provincial office in the final weeks before the deadline.

13.2.2 Training and Technical Assistance

Training is one of the most valuable services the CSEA offers – and one of the most underutilized. Many cooperatives in BARMM do not know they can request training from the CSEA. They wait for announcements or invitations that may never come. Do not make this mistake.

Pre-Registration Seminars (PRS). The CSEA conducts or accredits institutions to conduct PRS for prospective cooperative members.35 The PRS is a mandatory requirement before registration. It covers cooperative principles, governance, financial management, and legal obligations. Your organizing group cannot register without PRS certificates for all members.

Officer and management training. The CSEA develops and delivers management training programs for elected officers, board members, committee members, and hired management staff.36 These programs cover:

  • Governance training – board responsibilities, fiduciary duties, conflict of interest rules
  • Financial management – bookkeeping, auditing standards, fund allocation
  • Operations management – business planning, supply chain, marketing
  • Compliance training – CAPR/CAFS preparation, COC requirements, reporting obligations

Technical assistance. Beyond formal training programs, the CSEA provides on-site technical assistance through its Provincial Offices and Cooperative Development Specialists.37 Technical assistance includes:

  • Business planning support – helping your cooperative develop or refine its business plan
  • Financial management advisory – reviewing your books, recommending improvements
  • Governance improvement – assessing your board’s effectiveness, recommending structural changes
  • Market linkages – connecting your cooperative with buyers, suppliers, and partner organizations

How to request training. Write a formal letter addressed to the CSEA Executive Director (for central office programs) or the Provincial Director (for provincial programs). State the specific training you need, the number of participants, your preferred schedule, and your cooperative’s registration number. Attach your latest COR and COC. Follow up within two weeks if you do not receive a response.

Partnership training programs. The CSEA also delivers training through partnerships with other agencies and organizations. One notable example is the 3ZERO Initiative – a partnership with the Ministry of Agriculture, Fisheries, and Agrarian Reform (MAFAR), the World Food Programme (WFP), and ACTED – which conducts the Agri-Fishery Cooperatives’ Partners Forum for sustainable and inclusive agriculture. Watch for announcements of these partnership programs. They often provide training at no cost to participating cooperatives.

13.2.3 Financial Assistance Programs (PESO Project, LAKAS SE)

The Bangsamoro Development Plan 2023-2028 identifies two flagship programs under the CSEA that provide direct financial and technical support to cooperatives.38 Understand these programs. If your cooperative qualifies, they can be transformative.

The Development of Cooperatives and Social Enterprise Program. This program aims to strengthen cooperative capacities to ensure profitability and functionality.39 It includes two projects:

The PESO Project (Profitability and Equity Support Oriented). PESO provides financial and technical assistance to improve cooperative profitability.40 The name captures the program’s dual focus: it is not just about giving money (financial assistance) but about building the internal capacity (equity support) that makes the money productive. PESO targets cooperatives that are registered and operational but struggling with profitability – exactly the challenge the BDP 2023-2028 identifies as the central problem facing Bangsamoro cooperatives.

The LAKAS SE Program (Lakbay Aral sa Social Enterprise). LAKAS SE is a learning program for social enterprise development.41 It provides structured learning experiences – including study tours, peer exchanges, and mentoring – for cooperatives and social enterprises. If your cooperative is exploring the social enterprise pathway discussed in earlier chapters, LAKAS SE is the program that will build your SE capability.

The Combatant Transitional Program. This program is covered in detail in Section 13.5. It includes two projects – the STOCK Project and the Cooperative Financial and Material Support Project – designed specifically for cooperatives organized by former combatants.

How to access these programs. Contact your CSEA Provincial Office and ask about the current call for applications. Financial assistance programs typically have eligibility criteria, application windows, and documentation requirements. Prepare the following before you apply:

  1. Updated COR and COC – your registration and compliance must be current
  2. Audited financial statements – the CSEA will assess your cooperative’s financial health
  3. Business plan – you must demonstrate how the financial assistance will be used and how it will improve profitability
  4. Board resolution – your board must formally authorize the application
  5. Training records – evidence that your officers and members have completed relevant CSEA training programs

Practical tip. Programs like PESO and LAKAS SE have limited funding. They prioritize cooperatives that demonstrate compliance, governance quality, and clear business plans. The cooperatives that get funded are not the ones with the biggest problems. They are the ones that have done the preparatory work and can show they will use the assistance productively. Start preparing now, even if the current call has closed.

13.3 The Role of LGU Cooperative Development Officers

Your LGU Cooperative Development Officer (CDO) is the government official closest to your cooperative’s daily operations. The CDO works at the municipal, city, or provincial level – right where your cooperative operates. A strong relationship with your CDO makes everything easier: registration support, compliance guidance, access to local programs, and coordination with the CSEA.

13.3.1 Functions Under RA 11535 and BTA Bill 210, Section 20

The legal basis. Republic Act No. 11535 (2021) mandated all LGUs to appoint Cooperative Development Officers.42 BTA Bill 210, Section 20 reinforces this mandate and adds functions specific to the Bangsamoro context.43 Together, these two laws define what your CDO is supposed to do.

The CDO’s eight core functions under BTA Bill 210. Section 20 enumerates the following functions:44

1. Facilitate organizing strategies. Your CDO helps communities organize cooperatives. If your barangay wants to form a cooperative but does not know where to start, the CDO is the first person to approach. The CDO assists with organizing meetings, identifying potential members, and connecting the group with PRS providers.

2. Provide customized guidance and technical assistance. The CDO provides cooperative-specific advice on financial efficiency, operational effectiveness, and market responsiveness.45 This is not generic advice. The CDO should know your cooperative’s business, your members’ challenges, and your local market conditions.

3. Assess and monitor performance. The CDO assesses cooperative performance and advises on compliance with laws and regulations.46 The results of these assessments must be reported to the CSEA. This means your CDO is both your advisor and a monitoring channel. Cooperate fully with performance assessments. They benefit you more than the CSEA – the assessment findings tell you what to fix before the CSEA inspection arrives.

4. Coordinate capacity building. The CDO coordinates with the CSEA, relevant agencies, and private sector organizations for capacity-building activities and access to funding.47 Think of the CDO as your broker for external resources. The CDO knows what programs are available – CSEA training, MAFAR agricultural programs, MTIT market linkages, MSSD social protection programs – and can connect your cooperative to the right opportunities.

5. Implement specialized programs. The CDO initiates and implements programs in line with the Master Plan for Cooperative Development in the Bangsamoro, with emphasis on sustainability and resilience.48

6. Submit biannual reports. The CDO compiles and submits comprehensive biannual reports to the CSEA about the progress, challenges, opportunities, and evolving needs of cooperatives in the jurisdiction.49 Your cooperative’s data feeds into these reports. Accurate reporting by the CDO leads to better-targeted CSEA programs for your area.

7. Foster strategic partnerships. The CDO facilitates partnerships among cooperatives, industry stakeholders, and other relevant organizations to promote cooperation, mutual support, and collective growth.50

8. Additional functions. The CDO performs additional functions as prescribed by the CSEA, the LGU, and other relevant laws, with focus on emerging trends and the dynamic needs of the cooperative sector.51

The problem. The BDP 2023-2028 identifies a critical gap: most LGUs in the Bangsamoro Region have no appointed Municipal Cooperative Officers.52 Regional cooperative officers find it difficult to coordinate at the LGU level without municipal counterparts. This is one of the root causes of the 11.66 percent compliance rate. Where there is no CDO, cooperatives are essentially on their own.

If your municipality has not yet appointed a CDO, raise the issue with your local government. RA 11535 makes the appointment mandatory. A letter from your cooperative to the municipal mayor – citing the law and requesting the appointment – is a legitimate and constructive step.

13.3.2 How to Work Effectively with Your LGU-CDO

Having a CDO is one thing. Working effectively with them is another. Here are practical guidelines:

Introduce your cooperative formally. Send a courtesy letter to the CDO after registration. Attach your COR, list of officers, and a brief description of your business. This establishes you as a cooperative that communicates professionally.

Include the CDO in your General Assembly. Invite the CDO as a resource person or observer at your annual and special general assemblies. The CDO gains firsthand knowledge of your cooperative’s condition, and you gain access to real-time government guidance.

Share your CAPR and CAFS. Give the CDO a copy of your annual reports. The CDO needs this data for the biannual report to the CSEA. And the CDO can spot compliance issues in your reports before the CSEA does – giving you time to correct them.

Request assistance in writing. When you need help – training, market linkages, technical advice – put the request in writing. A written request creates a record, triggers a formal response obligation, and shows the CDO’s office that cooperative demand exists. Verbal requests are easily forgotten.

Attend CDO-organized events. When the CDO organizes cooperative summits, compliance drives, or training sessions, show up. Active cooperatives get prioritized for resources. Absent cooperatives get overlooked.

Coordinate, do not depend. Your CDO is a partner, not your manager. Your cooperative must maintain its own records, meet its own deadlines, and build its own capacity. The CDO supports your efforts. The CDO does not replace them.

13.4 The Broader Cooperative Ecosystem

Beyond the CSEA and the LGU-CDO, your cooperative exists within a broader ecosystem of legislative oversight bodies, national agencies, development partners, and civil society organizations. Knowing these actors helps you access resources, anticipate policy changes, and build alliances.

13.4.1 The Committee on Cooperatives and Social Enterprises (BTA Parliament)

The Bangsamoro Transition Authority (BTA) Parliament exercises legislative oversight over the cooperative sector through its committee system. The committee responsible for cooperative legislation reviews bills, conducts hearings, and recommends policy measures that directly affect your cooperative.

What this committee does for your cooperative. The committee reviews and deliberates on proposed legislation affecting cooperatives and social enterprises – including BTA Bill 210, the proposed Bangsamoro Cooperative and Social Enterprise Code that is referenced throughout this guidebook.53 When this bill is enacted, it becomes the primary legal framework for your cooperative.

The committee also conducts oversight hearings to assess how well the CSEA is performing its mandate. If your cooperative has systemic concerns about CSEA services – slow processing, unresponsive provincial offices, unclear regulations – these concerns can be raised during oversight hearings. Your cooperative federation or union is the appropriate channel for communicating these concerns to the committee.

How you connect to Parliament. You do not engage the committee directly as an individual cooperative. Engagement happens through your federation or union, through the CSEA (which is regularly called to committee hearings), or through formal consultations that the committee conducts with the cooperative sector. When the committee announces public hearings on cooperative legislation, attend or submit a written position paper. Your voice matters during the legislative process – not after the law is passed.

13.4.2 CDA and National Government Coordination

The Cooperative Development Authority (CDA) is the national government agency that regulates cooperatives across the Philippines under Republic Act 9520 (Philippine Cooperative Code of 2008).54 The relationship between the CDA and the CSEA is defined by the Bangsamoro Organic Law and the transition process.

Before BARMM. In the former ARMM, the CDA was the sole agency mandated to register cooperatives. CDA policies and guidelines governed all cooperative activities in the region.

The transition. With the establishment of BARMM, cooperative regulation transitioned from the CDA to the CSEA. The CSEA is now in its transition phase – building institutional capacity, establishing provincial offices, and developing Bangsamoro-specific policies.55 During this transition, several practical realities affect your cooperative:

  • Cooperatives registered under the CDA continue to be recognized. You do not need to re-register with the CSEA immediately. However, watch for transitory provisions once BTA Bill 210 is enacted – these may require re-registration or compliance updates within a specified period.
  • National cooperative policies still apply in areas not yet covered by Bangsamoro-specific regulations. RA 9520 remains in force as the baseline law. BTA Bill 210 will supplement and, in some areas, supersede RA 9520 upon enactment.
  • CDA training and programs may still be available to BARMM cooperatives, particularly those operated by national government agencies or funded by national programs.

What the CSEA uniquely has. A provision-by-provision legal analysis reveals that the CSEA possesses several powers and institutional features that the CDA does not have.65 These unique features reflect the Bangsamoro’s distinct constitutional status:

CSEA Unique Feature Significance
Social enterprise jurisdiction The CDA’s authority covers cooperatives only. The CSEA regulates both cooperatives and social enterprises – a broader developmental scope.66
Organic-law authority The CDA derives its authority from ordinary legislation (RA 11364). The CSEA’s authority traces to the Bangsamoro Organic Law – a constitutionally mandated enactment with higher normative weight.67
Islamic banking and Shari’ah integration The BOL empowers the Bangsamoro Government to establish Islamic banking and Shari’ah-compliant financial institutions (BOL Art. XIII, Secs. 29-30). The CDA’s national framework does not address Islamic cooperative finance.68
Express poverty reduction mandate BAA 13 positions cooperatives as platforms for poverty reduction. The CDA’s statutory language focuses on cooperatives as instruments of equity and economic development – without the same targeted poverty reduction framing.69
Asymmetric intergovernmental status The CSEA is an autonomous institution within an asymmetric governmental framework. CDA regional offices are merely administrative extensions of the national agency.70

What the CDA has that the CSEA currently lacks. The comparative analysis also reveals critical powers the CDA exercises under RA 11364 and RA 9520 that the CSEA does not yet possess.71 These gaps create regulatory voids that affect your cooperative:

CDA Power Impact of CSEA Gap
Quasi-judicial dispute resolution The CDA hears and decides inter- and intra-cooperative disputes, with decisions appealable to the Court of Appeals. The CSEA currently lacks a dedicated regional dispute forum.
Cease and desist orders The CDA can immediately halt harmful cooperative activities. The CSEA cannot issue cease and desist orders.
Suspension, cancellation, dissolution, liquidation The CDA can terminate non-compliant cooperatives. The CSEA cannot currently order dissolution of a non-compliant cooperative.
Conservatorship The CDA appoints conservators for illiquid cooperatives to protect member deposits. The CSEA lacks this rehabilitation mechanism.
Training institution accreditation The CDA accredits non-academic cooperative training institutions. The CSEA cannot standardize training quality through accreditation.
Capital assessment authority The CDA periodically adjusts paid-up capital requirements. The CSEA cannot calibrate financial thresholds for cooperatives.
Apex organization recognition The CDA recognizes apex organizations and a national cooperative alliance. The CSEA lacks authority to recognize hierarchical cooperative structures.
Merger, consolidation, and division jurisdiction The CDA processes structural changes. The CSEA cannot adjudicate mergers or consolidations.

Why BTA Bill 210 is critical. The passage of BTA Bill 210 (the Bangsamoro Cooperative and Social Enterprise Code) is the most important legislative priority for closing these gaps.72 Until its enactment, the CSEA’s regulatory authority remains incomplete relative to the CDA. Your cooperative should monitor this bill’s progress through the BTA Parliament. Its enactment will transform the CSEA from an agency with strong organic-law foundations but limited enforcement tools into a fully empowered regulatory and developmental authority.

Coordination mechanisms. The CSEA coordinates with the CDA on matters of national policy, data sharing, and cooperative master listing. If your cooperative operates across regions – for example, a Cotabato-based cooperative with suppliers in Region XII – you may need to engage both the CSEA and the CDA. In such cases, ask your CSEA Provincial Office for guidance on the jurisdictional boundaries.

13.4.3 Development Partners, NGOs, and Civil Society Organizations

The Bangsamoro cooperative sector benefits from significant support by development partners, non-governmental organizations (NGOs), and civil society organizations (CSOs). These organizations provide funding, technical assistance, training, and market access that complement government programs.

Key categories of partners.

International development organizations. Several international organizations have cooperative development programs in BARMM. The World Food Programme (WFP) partners with the CSEA and MAFAR on the 3ZERO Initiative, which supports agri-fishery cooperatives through the Agri-Fishery Cooperatives’ Partners Forum.56 Other international partners operate in areas such as livelihood development, conflict transformation, and economic resilience.

National NGOs and CSOs. Philippine-based organizations provide cooperative training, community organizing, and social enterprise development services in the Bangsamoro.
